text: BENBECULA, one of the Hebrides, lying between the islands of North and South Uist, from the last of which it is separated by a narrow channel nearly dry at low water. It is a low flat island, measuring about 8 or 9 m. each way. The soil is sandy and unproductive. In the interior are several fresh water lakes; and its shores are indented with an endless variety of bays, and fringed with islands. "The sea," says Macculloch, "is here all islands, and the land all lakes. That which is not rock is sand; that which is not mud is bog; that which is not bog is lake; and that which is not lake is sea; and the whole is a labyrinth of islands, peninsulas, promontories, bays, and channels."
BENBO, a mountain, 13 m. W of Manor-Hamilton, co. Leitrim. Alt. 1,500 ft.
BENBREDA, a mountain, SE of Dungiven, in co. Londonderry. Alt. 1,531 ft.
BENBULBEN, a mountain 6 m. N of Sligo, in co. Sligo. Alt. 1,697 ft.
BENBURB, a village in the p. of Clonfeacle, co. Tyrone, on the l. bank of the Blackwater, 3 m. SW of Moy. Pop. 330.
BENCAIRN, a mountain in Kirkcudbrightshire, in the p. of Rerwick, rising 1,200 ft. above sea-level.
BENCHOCHAN, a mountain in the p. of Aberfoyle, Perthshire, rising to the height of 3,000 ft. above sea-level.
BENCHONZIE, a mountain of Perthshire, in the N extremity of the p. of Monivaird, having an alt., according to Jameson, of 2,923 ft.

BENCLOCH, or BENCLEUGH, the highest of the Ochils, in the p. of Tillicoultry, Clackmannanshire. It is mostly composed of granite, containing large crystals of black schorl. It attains an alt. of 2,420 ft. above the level of the Devon, which runs at its base.

BENCLYBRIC, or Beinchlibrig, the highest mountain in Sutherlandshire, on the skirts of the parishes of Lairg and Farr. Its form is conical, and its alt. about 3,200 ft. BENCOOLEN, -or more correctly, according to native orthography and pronunciation, BENKAHULEE -a town and district in the island of Sumatra, in S lat. 3° 49′ 16", E long. 102° 16′ 15", which, until the year 1825, was held by the East India company, and administratively incorporated with the presidency of Bengal, whence a civil officer was deputed to administer the government. Its area, under the English sway, was about 10 sq. m.; and the pop., chiefly of the native tribe called Rejangs, was estimated at 20,000. The official name of the fort and town while in the English possession was Marlborough. The town was a petty and inconsiderable place, inhabited | pleted, and the governor made a speedy retreat to by a mixed pop. of natives of the country, Malays, Javanese, Chinese, natives of Bengal, Malabar, and Coromandel, with a trifling proportion of Europeans and their descendants. The soil around B. is a fine red vegetable mould, affording abundant produce; and the whole vegetable part of the creation is here on a magnificent scale. Almost every house in the settlement had a garden and an orchard, and some of the residents erected large and commodious country seats. Coal has been found in the neighbourhood, but turned to no account from the want of water carriage. The medium heat throughout the year is about 81° or 82°. Tigers are very destructive, frequently issuing from the thickets and carrying off men and beasts; and notwithstanding the rewards offered by the British government, these were seldom claimed. Sheep and cows are scarce; but the buffalo is a useful substitute in supplying the inhabitants with milk, butter, and meat, and in promoting agricultural operations. The feathered tribes are numerous; and plenty of good poultry can be easily procured. When Sir Stamford Raffles was appointed lieutenant gov. of B. in 1817, he found the settlement in a state of ruin and dilapidation: the roads impassable,—the highways in the town overrun with rank grass,—the government-house "a den of ravenous dogs and pole-cats!" The nature of the government, as administered under the English at B., had been even more faulty and defective than that of any other branch of the Indian local government. A number of feudal chiefs were nominally recognised as the sovereigns of the country; and the Sumatran laws were administered to the people; but the authority of the resident and his agents was perpetually interposed in a manner defined neither by law nor custom. The settlement, in fact, was consigned to that neglect which usually attends an unprofitable and useless establishment. The principal local revenues of the government were in the gaming and cock-fighting farms, the continuance of which Sir Stamford instantly denounced as "destructive of every principle of good government, of social order, and the morals of the people." His first act was to abolish these farms, and to emancipate the Caffre slaves whom he found in the settlement. He then classified about 500 convicts who had been sent to B. from Bengal, according to character; and in a short time converted them into useful labourers, and happy members of society. He next assembled the native chiefs, and pledged himself before them to administer the government according to equity, justice, and good policy; and likewise declared that they should be at liberty to undertake the cultivation of pepper, or not, as they pleased. On his arrival no one thought of living out of the settlement, or venturing a distance of 2 or 3 m. from the walls of the fort after sunset: Sir Stamford, however, immediately built a country-house for himself 12 m. from the fort, and in five years the whole intermediate space was chequered with villas and surrounded with plantations, and of 100,000 nutmeg trees which had been planted on his assuming the reins of government, one-fourth were in full bearing. He also established a plan of schools for educating the whole of the native pop. All these humane and enlightened measures were rendered in a great measure abortive by the surrender of B. to the Dutch in 1825.—The English first settled here in 1685, after they were compelled to quit Batavia; and they built a fort in 1690, which they called Fort York. A mortality, which swept off the governor and the whole council, having occurred in 1693, the situation of the fort was changed, and a new one erected, in 1719. To this the natives set fire before it was com- Batavia. A compromise having however taken place, the fort was completed, and called Marlborough. This settlement was taken by the French in 1760, and the fort dismantled. It was captured a second time, at a later period, when 315 tons of pepper were destroyed in the warehouses. During the five years preceding its cession to the Dutch, the excess of expenditure over revenue amounted to about £85,000; and the total annual traffic did not exceed 400,000 dollars.—Marsden.—Crawford.—Life of Sir S. Raffles.
BENCOWAZ, a town of Austria, in Dalmatia, SE of Zara.

BENCRUACHAN, a magnificent mountain of Argyleshire, in the district of Lorn, between Loch Etive and Loch Awe. It has an elevation of 3,393 ft. according to Jameson, or 3,390 ft. according to an admeasurement of Colonel Watson; and its base is 20 m. in circuit. Its steepest side is towards the NE; from the south it rises gently, and may be ascended with considerable ease. Macculloch says of it: "Compared to Benlomond it is a giant; and its grasp is no less gigantic. From the bold granite precipices of its sharp and rugged summit—which is literally a point—we look down its red and furrowed sides into the upper part of Loch Etive and over this magnificent group of mountains, which, extending northward and eastward, display one of the finest landscapes of mere mountains in the Highlands. Its commanding position not only enables us thus to bring under our feet the whole of this group as far as Appin and Glenco, and even to Bennevis, but opens a view of the whole of the eastern ocean of mountains, reaching from Rannoch as far as Benlawers and Benlomond, and, beyond them, to lands which only cease to be visible because they at length blend with the sky. So marked also are their characters, so rocky and precipitous their summits, and so varied their forms, that this landscape excels, in variety as in picturesque character, all other landscapes of mere mountains, excepting perhaps that from Benlair in Ross-shire. The view which it yields of the opener country, is not much inferior to that from Benlawers, if indeed it is inferior; and, in this respect, it can only be compared with that mountain and Benlomond." Lord Teignmouth remarks that this mountain derives a crater-like appearance from the semicircular shape of its huge bare buttresses." According to Dr. Macculloch, the masses of granite at its summit, and the porphyritic veins which intersect the granite, affect the magnet.
BENDAH, a town of Persia, in Farsistan, at the influx of the Khabalon into the Persian gulf.
BENDALA, a town of Sudan, in Bornu, in N lat. 11° 20′.
BENDEARG, a mountain in Athole, in Perthshire. Alt. 3,550. BENDEKIL, BUNDEKEEL, or BENHUDAL, a village of Persia, in Khuzistan, at the junction of the Ab-i-Diz, or river of Diz, with the Karun.
BENDELKEND. See BUNDLEKUND.

BENDEMIR, a river of Persia, in Farsistan, which rises in the Bakhtiyari mountains, on the confines of Irak Ajemi; runs from NW to SE, and receives two branches of the Kurab, or Cyrus, in the vale of Merdasht, to the E of the v. of Bendamir; and then continues a SE course to the lake of Bakhtegan—the Salsus lacus—50 m. ESE of Shiraz, into which it flows after a course of more than 200 m., measured from the source of the Kurab the longer and larger stream. It is the Araxes which Alexander crossed on his march to Persepolis [Curtius, v. 5].—The v. of B. consists of 60 houses, with 21 water-mills erected on the river; and here is the famous bend or dyke constructed in the 10th cent. by BEN Amir Uzun-Deylemi, consisting of a flat bridge of 13 arches, beneath which the river forms a beautiful cascade. As the bed of the river is deep, seven other dykes have been constructed in its lower course for the irrigation of the fields; but the environs of the v. are uncultivated because the dyke is out of repair, and the water does not therefore rise enough for the purposes of irrigation in this quarter. De Bode, though he did not find roses hanging over "the calm Bendemeer," observes that "it must be a delightful spot when embosomed in the transparent foliage of its gardens, with its picturesque cascades, and the fantastically shaped rocks which overhang the village."

BENDER, a small but strong and regularly fortified town of European Russia, in Bessarabia, on the r. bank of the Dniester, the cap. of the rajah or territory of the same name; in N lat. 46° 50' 40", E long. 29° 36'; 40 m. SE of Kijenef; 100 m. E of Jassy. Pop. 4,700. It is built close by the river, in the form of a crescent, and has a strong citadel on an eminence. On account of the importance attached to its position, the command of this place, when it belonged to the Turks, was commonly intrusted to a pasha of three tails. Charles of Sweden, after the disastrous battle of Pultowa, in 1709, put himself under the protection of the Turks, and having made a rapid retreat, pitched his camp on the Dniester, opposite to B., but soon after removed it to the other side, where it remained till 1711. In that year it was transported to the neighbouring village of Kirnitza, where it was attacked two years afterwards by a body of Turks; and the king taken prisoner, after an obstinate engagement. In 1770, B. was taken by storm, by the Russians, under Panim, who butchered the greater part of the inhabitants-at that time amounting to 30,000 individuals, nearly half of whom were soldiers-and reduced the town to ashes. At the peace of Kanardgi it was restored to the Turks; but on the 15th Nov. 1789, the Russians again took possession of the place, almost without firing a shot. They again restored it, at the peace of Jassy; but finally made themselves masters of it a third time, and retained it at the peace of 1812. At the period of Marshal Marmont's visit, in 1834, this fortress was undergoing a thorough repair. On the l. bank of the river, and nearly 3 m. from B., there is another fortress which was constructed by the Russians when the Pruth was their frontier.
BENDER ABASSI. See GOMBRUN.
BENDER-ASSELN, a port of Persia, on the Persian gulf, 85 m. W of Lar.
BENDER-BUSHIRE. See BUSHIRE.
BENDER-DELUN, a town of Persia, in Farsistan, 130 m. W of Shiraz, at the NW extremity of the Persian gulf.

BENDER-EREKLI. See EREKLI. The word Bender signifies 'a port' or 'harbour' in Persian; and its diminutive is Benderaki, 'a little port.' Hence its frequent occurrence as a prefix in Persian topography.
BENDER-RIG, a town of Persia, in Farsistan, on the NE side of the Persian gulf, 35 m. N of Bushire, in N lat. 29° 22'. Pop. 1,500.
BENDESDORF, a town of Austria, in Siebenburgen, to the N of Carlsburg.
BEND-MALIN, a town of Persia, in Farsistan, on the coast of the Persian gulf, within the government of the imam of Muscat.
BENDOCHY, a parish in Perthshire, skirted on the E by the river Isla. Pop. in 1801, 860; in 1841, 783.
BENDORF, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of the Rhine, circle and 4 m. N of Coblentz, on the r. bank of the Rhine. Pop. in 1837, 2,126.
BENDUSZ, a town of Austrian Poland, in the circle of Kielce, obwod of Olkusz. Pop. 600.
BENDZIN, a town of Austrian Poland, in the circle of Kielce, obwod of Olkusz. Pop. 2,300.

BENE, a town of Piedmont, in the district of Coni, in the prov. and 10 m. N of Mondovi. It is situated on a hill between the Stura and Tanaro; and is defended by an ancient castle of considerable strength. It contains a collegiate church, a communal college, two convents and an hospital. A species of orgeat, made in large quantities in this town, is exported to various parts of Italy. Pop. 5,600.
BENE-ABBAS, a dist. and town of Tripoli, at the NE extremity of the Gharian mountains, 42 m. S of Tripoli.
BENEDEK (Sr.), a town of Hungary, in the comitat and 9 m NNE of Bars, on the r. bank of the Grau.
BENEDEK (SZENT), or BENDERSDORF, a town of Transylvania, in the circle of Unter-Alba, 10 m. N of Kartsburg. It contains a Protestant church. The pop. consists of Hungarians and Wallachians.
BENEDEN, a parish in Kent, 3 m. SE of Cranbrook. Area 6,780 acres. Pop. 1,594.

BENEDETTO (SAN), a town of Piedmont, in the dist. of Coni, in the prov. and 16 m. SSE of Alba, near the l. bank of the Bormida.—Also a town in the States-of-the-Church, in the deleg. and 12 m. WNW of Ascoli, and 3 m. N of Porto d'Ascoli, on the Adriatic.
BENEDETTO-IN-ALPE (SAN), a village of Tuscany, in the campanate of Florence, 3 m. SW of Portico. It formerly contained a richly endowed Benedictine convent. Pop. 770.

BENEDETTO-DI-POLIRONE, a town and port of Venetian Lombardy, in the prov. and 10 m. SE of Mantua, near the r. bank of the Po. It formerly contained a Benedictine abbey, which was the richest in Italy. Pop. 1,000.

BENEDICTBEUERN, a hamlet of Bavaria, in the circle of Upper Bavaria, 15 m. SW of Tolz, near the NE extremity of the Kochel-see, at an alt. of 616 metres above sea-level. It is celebrated for its glasshouse belonging to the Optical institute of Munich, and has mineral springs and baths. It formerly possessed a noble Benedictine abbey, founded in 733, and one of the most ancient in Germany. The environs contain quarries of fine marble.
BENEFIELD, a parish in Northamptonshire, 4 m. W of Oundle. Area 5,100 acres. Pop. 533.
BENEJACQ, a commune of France, in the dep. of Basses-Pyrenees, and cant. of Clarac-pres-Nay. Pop. 1,617.
BENERN, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of Saxony and circle of Erfurth, on the Laine. Pop. 1,000.

BENESCHAU, or Benessow, a town of Bohemia, in the circle and 31 m. ESE of Beraun, and 24 m. SSE of Prague.—Also a town of Prussia, in the prov. of Silesia, in the circle and 13 m. SSW of Ratibor, near the l. bank of the Oppa. It contains a castle and a Catholic church. Pop. 400. BENESCHAU, BENESSOW. See BENSEN.

BENEST, a commune of France, in the dep. of Charente, and cant. of Champagne-Mouton. It was the scene of the victory of Charlemagne over the Saracens, in commemoration of which he founded a church in the village, and bestowed upon it privileges which it continued to enjoy till the Revolution. The dist. affords large quantities of chestnuts.
BENET, a commune of France, in the dep. of Vendee, and cant. of Mailbezais. It has some manufactories of coarse linen. Pop. 2,233.

BENETUTTI, a parish and village of the island of Sardinia, in the prov. and 13 m. NW of Nuoro, and 47 m. ESE of Sassari. Pop. 1,517. The surrounding district is mountainous, and abounds in luxuriant pasturage. To the W of the village is the small river of the same name. On reaching Sedilo, it takes the appellation of Oristano.

BENEVENT, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Creuse, arrond. of Bourganeuf. The cant. comprises 12 com., and contained in 1831 a pop. of 9,365. The town is 15 m. NNW of Bourganeuf, and contains manufactories of linen and cordage. It has a considerable trade in cattle, skins, and toys. Pop. 1,422.

BENEVENTE, a town of Brazil, in the prov. and 50 m. SSW of Espiritu Santo. It is advantageously situated at the mouth of the river of the same name, which forms a good harbour. The adjacent district is fertile, and produces cotton, sugar, rice, &c. The pop. consists chiefly of aborigines.

BENEVE'NTO, a duchy of Italy, in the Principato Ultra of the kingdom of Naples, but belonging for many centuries to the States-of-the-Church, and forming a delegation or distretto governativo of the Papal dominions. It consists of the city of B. and a small district around it, in the valley of Beneventana; and comprises, besides the city, nine villages and hamlets, and a superficial area of 139,191 tavole quadrato. Pop. in 1833, 23,040. It is a hilly district; but has an active trade in cattle, and produces excellent fruit and wine, with a tolerable crop of corn, and abounds in game. The revenue it yields to the Pope is about 6,000 dollars a-year.—This prov. anciently formed part of the country of the Samnites, and was erected into a duchy by the Lombards, in 571, in favour of Zotto, one of their leaders. His successor Arechis. conquered almost the whole of the country which now forms the kingdom of Naples. The fall of the Lombard kingdom did not affect the state of B., which long maintained its independence, and spread from Samnium, its original seat, over half the modern kingdom of Naples, until intestine commotions, in conjunction with powerful assaults from abroad, hastened its downfall. It fell successively into the hands of the Saracens and Normans. In 1059 Robert Guiscard having conquered Calabria, propitiated the Holy See by granting to it the city and district of B. It continued thenceforth in the possession of the papal government, notwithstanding frequent attempts made by the kings of Naples to incorporate it with their dominions. The last and most remarkable of these took place in 1769, when it was retained for upwards of five years. Bonaparte made these disputes a pretext for seizing on the duchy in 1806, when he raised it to a principality, and conferred it on his minister, Talleyrand, who received about 14,000 ducats annually from it, after paying the expenses of the government. Benevento, the capital of the above delegation, is situated on an eminence, beneath a lofty chain of hills, between two narrow valleys, in one of which runs the Sabbato, and in the other the Calore, which unite soon after into one stream, 32 m. NE of Naples, 125 ESE of Rome, in N lat. 41° 7'. It is surrounded by walls, and defended by a castle. It is well-built, and contains a fine town-hall, and a theological seminary with a good library. The town contained 8 parish churches, 12 convents for monks, and 2 for nuns, with 16,316 inhabitants in 1830. The number was in former times somewhat more considerable, but was reduced by the plague in 1656, and by a dreadful earthquake which happened on the 6th of June 1688. Cardinal Orsini—at that time archbishop, and afterwards elected pope, in 1724, under the name of Benedict XIII. -met with the singular accident of falling from the uppermost to the lowest apartment of the archiepiscopal palace, when it was destroyed by the earthquake, but was dug out alive, and contributed greatly to the rebuilding of the city. No place in Italy, with the exception of Rome, contains so many interesting remains of antiquity as B.; scarcely a yard of wall in it is built of any thing but fragments of altars, tombs, columns, and entablatures. These antiquities have been amply described by Giovanni de Vita, in his work entitled Antiquitates Beneventana. Among other remains, the arch, which was erected in honour of Trajan, A. D. 114, is still in good preservation, and forms one of the five gates of the city. According to Sir R. Colt Hoare, it "surpasses any of those in the imperial city of Rome;" and Craven pronounces it to be "perhaps the best specimen of Roman workmanship existing." There are also a ruined theatre, an obelisk, and fragments of a bridge. The cathedral is a vast but clumsy edifice, a mixture of the Saracenic and the Gothic style. The monastery of Santa Sofia presents a curious specimen of the architecture of the Lower Greek empire.—The origin of this city is involved in some obscurity, but there is no doubt of its having been one of the principal towns of the Samnites, and one in which they frequently took refuge when defeated by the Romans. It was at that time called Maleventum; but the Romans, on settling a colony there, changed the name to Beneventum. The neighbouring territory has been the scene of several military conflicts. According to some antiquaries, the celebrated Furca Caudina was near a v. called Le Forche d'Arpaja, a few miles from B. on the Appian way. Pyrrhus was defeated in the neighbourhood of B., by the consul Curius Dentatus. In a plain near this town, Charles of Anjou, king of Naples, defeated and killed his rival Manfred of Suabia, in 1266. B. has been the see of an archbishop since the year 969, and reckons St. Januarius among the number of its metropolitans; it has likewise given birth to three popes. It is governed by a cardinal sent from Rome, with the title of legate. Its archb. is metropolitan of Arrano, Ascogli-et-Cerignola, Avellino, Bojano, Bovino, Cerreta-Telese-et-Alife, Larino, Lucera, Santa-Agata-de-Goti, San-Severo, and Termoli.—To the E of B., about 3 m. from Frigento, is Virgil's valley and lake of Amsanctus.

BENFELD, or BENFELDEN, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the prov. of Lower Rhine, arrond. of Schelestat. The cant. comprises 15 com., and contained in 1831 a pop. of 15,416. The town, which was formerly fortified, is on the l. bank of the Ill, 10 m. NNE of Schelestat, and 18 m. SSW of Strasburg. It contains a government tobacco depot, and has some cotton manufactories. The environs produce hemp and tobacco. Pop. 2,230.
BENFIELD SIDE, a township in the p. of Lanchester, Durham, 131 m. NW of Durham. Area 2,150 acres. Pop. 1,074.
BENFLEET (North and South), two parishes in Essex, 3 m. W of Rayleigh. Area of North B. 1,730 acres. Pop. 364. Area of South B. 2,160 acres. Pop. 707.

BENGAL. Or Lower Bengal, as it is called, to distinguish it from the Upper or Western provinces of the presidency of Bengal, the most eastern province of Hindostan, one of the fifteen subahs into which that empire was divided in the reign of Acbar, and the seat of the supreme government of British India. It is bounded on the N by Nepal and Bhutan; on the E by a range of mountains which separate it from Arracan and the Birmese dominions; on the S by the bay of Bengal; and on the W by Orissa and Bahar. Its greatest length, from E to W, is about 350 m.; and tending from 21° 30' to 26° 40' N lat., and from 86° to 92° 30' E long. "The natural situation of B. is singularly happy with respect to security from the attacks of foreign enemies. On the N and E it has no warlike neighbours; and has, moreover, a formidable barrier of mountains, rivers, or extensive waters, towards those quarters, should such an enemy start up. On the S is a sea-coast gnarded by shallows and impenetrable woods, and with only one port-and even that of difficult access—in an extent of 300 m. It is on the W only that any enemy is to be apprehended; and, even there, the natural barrier is strong; and with its population and resources, aided by the usual proportion of British troops, in addition to the Sepoy establishment, B. might bid defiance to all that part of Hindostan which might find itself inclined to become its enemy." [Rennell's Memoir, p. cxv.] Name.] The term Bengal is derived from Banga, the original or Sanscrit name of the country, to which the Mussulman conquerors added their usual affix ul, calling it Bangul or Bangala, which Europeans write Bengal. According to the popular notion, all the Indian countries on the E of the Ganges are called Banga; but according to the Purani descriptions, the term is strictly applicable only to those regions lying immediately to the N of the Ganges. [Ram Comulsen. Area and Divisions.] Major Rennell estimates the area of this prov. at 97,244 sq. m. It is divided into seventeen districts or collectorates: viz.- 1. Backergunge, or Bacargany, at the SE extremity of the delta of the Ganges. Area 2,780 sq. m. Pop. in 1822, 686,640. 2. BIRBHUM, or VIRABHUMI, at the NW extremity of the prov. Area 3,870 sq. m. Pop. 1,267,065. 3. Burdwan, or Berdhwan, to the S of Birbhum. Area 2,000 sq. m. Pop. 1,187,580. 4. CHITTAGONG, OF CHATIGANG, the SE extremity of the prov. Area 2,980 sq. m. Pop. 700,800. 5. Hugli, or Hoogly, to the S of Burdwan. Area 2,260 sq. m. Pop. 1,239,150. 6. JESSORE, or JASAR, to the E of Hugli, on the embouchures of the Ganges. Area 5,180 sq. m. Pop. 1,183,590. 7. MYMANSINGH, or MAIMENSINGH, on the Brahmaputra, to the W of Silhet. Area 6,988 sq. m. Pop. 1,454,670. 8. Murshedabad, or Murshidabad, to the E of Birbhum, and S of the Ganges. Area 1,870 sq. m. Pop. 762,690. 9. NUDDEA, or NEDIVA, to the N of Calcutta. Area 3,105 sq. m. Pop. 1,187,160. 10. Purneah, or Purniya, comprehending a part of Bihar. Area 7,460 sq. m. Pop. 1,362,165. 11. Rajshahi, or Rajeshaye, on the Ganges, to the N of Birbhum. Area 3,950 sq. m. Pop. 4,087,155. 12. RUNGPORE, or RANGPUR, S of Bhutan, and W of Assam. Area 7,856 sq. m. Pop. 1,340,350. 13. SILHET, or SYLHET, at the E extremity of the prov. Area 3,532 sq. m.? Pop. 1,083,720. 14. TIPERA, TIPPERAH, or TIPURA, to the S of Silhet, and E of the Birmese dominions. Area 6,830 sq. m. Pop. 1,372,260. 15. THE TWENTY-FOUR PERGUNNAHS, to the S of Calcutta, and E of the Hugli. Area 3,610 sq. m.; and including Calcutta district 4,722 sq. m. Pop. 1,304,740. its greatest breadth, from N to S, about 300 m.; ex- | These districts are arranged into three divisions subordinate to Calcutta, Murshedabad, and Dacca, 1 as stated in our succeeding article on the presidency of B .- The principal cities, in the order of their pop., are Calcutta, Dacca, Murshedabad, Burdwan, Chandernagore, Purneah, Rajmahal, Dinajpur, Naraingunge, Malda, Gar, and Chandercona. General aspect.] The general aspect of B., except towards its E and W frontiers, is that of a champaign country, intersected by numerous rivers, surrounded by chains of lofty mountains, and descending imperceptibly towards the sea. That part of the delta through which the Ganges expands its branches as it approaches the sea, is the lowest district in the prov. and seems as if newly emerging from the waters. It is called the Soonderbun or 'Woods;' and is alone equal in extent to any of the three kingdoms of which Great Britain is composed. It is a labyrinth of creeks and rivers,-of jungle and stagnant water,a dreary uninhabited waste, infested by boars and tigers; but its numerous canals are so disposed as to form a complete inland navigation throughout the lower delta. This district yields salt equal to the whole consumption of B. and its dependencies; and furnishes an inexhaustible store of timber for domestic uses, and boat-building. Some attempts have been made, and with considerable success, to clear and cultivate this inhospitable tract; but as it is deemed by some a matter of policy to have such an extensive desert lying between our possessions and the only point of attack from an European enemy, there is not much encouragement given to the cultivation of the Sunderbunds. Within the boundaries of the prov., and particularly in the SW angle, and on the N of the Ganges, are to be found more elevated tracts of land, remarkable for picturesque scenery, and for the neat habitations of the peasantry. These upper regions, however—which are not liable to inundation, and which were formerly called Baréndra—are of inconsiderable extent, and of very inferior estimation in the views of commerce and finance. The principal physical division of B., and that which is most valuable for its produce and manufactures, is the extensive and uninterrupted level through which the Ganges and Brahmaputra slowly roll their immense volumes of water, and which they annually overflow in the rainy season. Rivers.] The Ganges and Brahmaputra, the two greatest rivers of India, flow through B., and have their embouchures in the bay of B. The prov. is further intersected in every direction by the affluents of these majestic streams, amongst which are the Cosi, the Conki, the Dummuda, and the Tista, all large rivers and tributaries to the Ganges; and the Jhinayi and Manas, tributaries to the Brahmaputra. The river-system of B. presents a singular interlacement or inosculation in the numerous irregular watercourses which have been from time to time formed in the soft alluvial flat, by the impetuous rush or weight of the swollen rivers during the season of inundation, and which unite nearly the whole watersystem of Lower B. by a series of natural canals, nearly all of which are navigable, even by large boats, in the rainy season. The jhils, or morasses, also at this season become navigable; and some of them are navigable throughout the year, marking the spot probably where river-channels once existed; for the hydrography of B. is in a state of constant fluctuation, such is the loose nature of the materials of which the soil is composed, on the one hand, and the prodigious power of the swollen rivers to plough-up new channels for themselves wherever a more tenacious part of their banks gives deflection to the current. Inundations.] The inundations, to which the province of B. in a manner owes its origin, and upon the due proportion of which its prosperity annually depends, necessarily form a most interesting object of attention to the natives, and must hold a | within the torrid zone, and borders on several extenprominent place in every account of the country. The following description of these periodical floods—the most distinct with which we are acquainted, and the least capable of abridgmentis submitted to our readers in the identical words of the eminent geographer from whose pen it proceeded. The Ganges "appears to owe its increase as much to the rain-water, that falls in the mountains contiguous to its source, and to the sources of the great northern rivers that fall into it, as to that which falls in the plains of Hindostan; for it rises 151 ft. out of 32 ft.—the sum total of its rising-by the latter end of June; and it is well-known that the rainy season does not begin in most of the flat countries till about that time. In the mountains, it begins early in April; and by the latter end of that month, when the rain-water has reached B., the rivers begin to rise, though by very slow degrees; for the increase is only about 1 inch per day for the first fortnight. It then gradually augments to 2 and 3 inches, before any quantity of rain falls in the flat countries; and, when the rain becomes general, the increase, at a medium, is 5 inches per day. By the latter end of July, all the lower parts of B., contiguous to the Ganges and Burrampooter, are overflowed; and form an inundation of more than 100 m. in width, nothing appearing but villages and trees, excepting very rarely the top of an elevated spot—the artificial mound of some deserted village—appearing like an island. But the inundations in B. are as much occasioned by the rain that falls there as by the waters of the Ganges; and, as a proof of it, the lands in general are overflowed to a considerable height, long before the bed of the river is filled. It must be remarked, that the ground adjacent to the river-bank, to the extent of some miles, is considerably higher than the rest of the country; and serves to separate the waters of the inundation from those of the river, until it overflows. This high ground is in some seasons covered a foot or more; but the height of the inundation within varies, of course, according to the irregularities of the ground; and is in some places 12 ft. Even when the inundation becomes general, the river still shows itself, as well by the grass and reeds on its banks, as by its rapid and muddy stream; for the water of the inundation acquires a blackish hue, by having been so long stagnant among grass and other vegetables; nor does it even lose this tinge, which is a proof of the predominancy of the rain-water over that of the river; as the slow rate of the motion of the inundation, (which does not exceed half-a-mile per hour,) is of the remarkable flatness of the country. There are particular tracts of lands, which, from the nature of their culture, and species of productions, require less moisture than others, and yet, by the lowness of their situation, would remain too long inundated, were they not guarded, by dikes or dams, from so copious an inundation as would otherwise happen from the great elevation of the surface of the river above them. Those dikes are kept up at an enormous expense; and yet do not always succeed, from want of tenacity in the soil of which they are composed. It is calculated that the length of those dikes collectively amounts to more than 1,000 English miles. Some of them, at the base, are equal to the thickness of an ordinary rampart. One particular branch of the Ganges—navigable only during the rainy season, but then equal to the Thames at Chelsea—is conducted between two of these dikes for about 70 m.; and when full, the passengers in the boats look down on the adjacent country as from an eminence. During the swollen state of the river, the tide totally loses its effect of counteracting the stream; and, in a great measure, that of ebbing and flowing, excepting near the sea. It is not uncommon for a strong wind, that blows up the river for any continuance, to swell the waters about 2 ft. above the ordinary level at that season, and such accidents have occasioned the loss of whole crops of rice. A very tragical event happened at Luckipour, in 1763, by a strong gale of wind conspiring with a high spring-tide, at a season when the periodical flood was within 12 foot of its highest pitch. It is said that the waters rose 6 ft, above the ordinary level. Certain it is, that the inhabitants of a considerable district, with their houses and cattle were totally swept away; and to aggravate their distress, it happened in a part of the country which scarcely produces a single tree for a drowning man to escape to. Embarkations of every kind traverse the inundation; those bound upwards, availing themselves of a direct course and still water, at a season when every stream rushes like a torrent. The wind, too, which at this season blows regularly from the SE, favours their progress; insomuch, that a voyage, which takes up nine or ten days by the course of the river, when confined within its banks, is now effected in six. Husbandry and grazing are both suspended; and the peasant traverses in his boat those fields, which, in another season, he was wont to plough; happy that the elevated site of the river banks places the herbage they contain within his reach; otherwise his cattle must perish." "The inundation is nearly at a stand for some days preceding the middle of August, when it begins to run off; for although great quantities of rain fall in the flat countries during August and September, yet, by a partial cessation of the rains in the mountains, there happens a deficiency in the supplies necessary to keep up the inundation." "The decrease of the inundation does not always keep pace with that of the river, by reason of the height of the banks; but after the beginning of October, when the rain has nearly ceased, the remainder of the inundation goes off quickly by evaporation, leaving the lands highly manured, and in a state to receive the seed, after the simple operation of ploughing." [Rennel's Memoir, p. 348, &c.] Climate.] As the prov. of B. lies almost entirely sive sandy wastes, it is subject to great extremes of heat; and has been accounted more unhealthy for Europeans than any other British settlement in India. The SE quarter especially, in which the town of Calcutta is situated, and which is a flat marshy country, was deemed at first almost as destructive as Batavia: and at one period, when the whole Europeans resident in Calcutta did not exceed 1,200, no fewer than 400 burials were numbered in six months. It is said that, under Mahommedan regime, this country was used as a place of exile for offenders of rank; and was considered to be dojakh, or an infernal region. [Ram Comulsen.] The great and general cause of disease in this country is an excess of bile, which occasions fevers, dysentery, inflammation of liver, with a long train of nervous affections. These diseases-which are most prevalent in the months of September and October—are generally very rapid in their progress, and chiefly attack those who are newly arrived from Europe; but the introduction of a more temperate and regular mode of living, and the more intimate acquaintance which the medical practitioners have acquired with the peculiar diseases of the country, have greatly contributed to render their attacks less frequent and fatal. Happily, the varieties of disease are not numerous, and their treatment is extremely uniform; almost every stage has its appropriate remedy, and nowhere are the prescriptions of the physician more certainly followed with success. By cutting canals, by draining the offensive marshes, and by clearing the ground of trees and jungle, the climate has been and it may be expected will be still farther improved; but it must always occasion a severe trial to every European constitution. Even those, who are not materially injured by its influence, are frequently rendered little capable of any exertion; and, during the hot season particularly, it is not uncommon to find the whole officers of a battalion, except one or two individuals, utterly unfit for duty, and this without any extraordinary or alarming complaint. This insalubrity is supposed to be owing, in a great measure, to the prevalence of the hot winds, which are occasionally loaded with sandy particles which are peculiarly pernicious to persons asleep, and frequently so suffocating as to be almost insupportable by the natives themselves; but may principally be attributed to the stagnant waters and putrescent substances which are left upon the flat surface of the country by the frequent inundations of its rivers. - The seasons are here commonly distinguished by the terms hot, cold, and rainy; but the natives subdivide them into six, comprising two months in each. The hot season continues from the beginning of March to the end of May; and during this period, the therm. very frequently rises to 100°, sometimes even to 110°; but in the middle parts of B. the extreme sultriness of the weather is greatly moderated by occasional thunder-storms, accompanied with rain or hail driven by sudden gusts of NW wind; while in the eastern districts, milder showers of rain are still more frequent, and peculiarly refreshing to the heated atmosphere. In the western districts contiguous to Bahar, a parching W wind continues during the greater part of the season. The rainy season commences in June, and lasts till October; and during the two first months, the rain is so heavy and constant, that frequently 3, 4, and even 5 inches of water have fallen in one day; but during the two last months of this season, there are frequent intermissions of rain, the weather is rather close and sultry, and thick unwholesome fogs prevail. The annual fall of rain varies from 70 to 80 inches. The cold season continues from November to February, during which period northerly winds prevail, the sky is clear and unclouded, and the weather generally pleasant to an European constitution. The dews during the night are abundant and penetrating, and greatly assist the progress of vegetation; while, in the more mountainous districts, even frost and extreme cold are frequently experienced. Soil.] The general soil of B. is a congeries of clay, mixed with a considerable portion of sand, fertilized by various salts, and by immense quantities of decayed vegetable and animal substances. It is a rich, blackish mould, extremely loose in its texture, of great depth, (6, 14, and even to 20 ft.,) lying on a bed of sand interspersed with shells and rotten wood, and affording every indication of a country gained from the sea, and formed by deposition from the waters of the rivers, and of the annual inundations. In proof of this supposition it may be mentioned, that there are frequently found, at the depth of 20 or 30 ft., the wrecks of boats, with their anchors and other implements, which seem to have been sunk in some remote period when the soil was lower, or when this vast plain formed the bottom of the sea. Husbandry.] The intense heats which succeed the rainy season, and which act upon the soil when full of moisture, produce, in B., a luxuriance of vegetation almost unknown in any other country in the world. The land is easily cultivated, and yields abundant crops, without any other manure than what has been deposited by the waters of the inundation. The principal food of the natives, -and, consequently, the principal object of the husbandman, - is rice; but very good wheat and barley, though much smaller and lighter than those grains in Europe, are also produced. The agricultural society of India, from enquiries circulated in 1843, ascertained that wheat could be landed in Calcutta at from 1 rupee to 2 rupees 12 annas, per maund of 82 lb., from different parts of the B. presidency; the cheapest being from the lower provinces, not only on account of their vicinity, but also from the cultivation being cheaper in consequence of the facility of irrigation from the returning inundations, especially on the banks of rivers. The greater dearness of the NW provinces,-where wheat is the principal object of cultivation,-is due not only to the greater distance and to the inhabitants themselves being large consumers of wheat, but also to the cultivation being more expensive in consequence of the occasional necessity for irrigating from wells. Canal-irrigation would much diminish this source of expense. Mr. Speede of Calcutta states the average price of wheat in Calcutta during 12 months to be 1r. 13a. 4p. This - which included the various profits of native merchants and brokers between the grower and the shipper-he deduced from actual sales of the best qualities, which alone were exported. The grain is dearest in September and October, or about the period of sowing the seed; and cheapest in March, when the crop has just been harvested. Taking the average price at 1r. 13a. 4p. per maund, and the rupee at 2s. (it is now only 1s. 9d.), then the price will be about 3s. 6d. for a maund of 82.28 lb. The average weight of a bushel of wheat being 60 lb., about 6 maunds will be required to make up a quarter of wheat, which will thus, on an average, cost about 21s. in Calcutta. A great variety of different kinds of pulse is raised during the intervals of attention to white grain, such as pease, chiches, pigeonpease, kidney-beans, &c.; and these constitute a very valuable article in B. husbandry, as they require little culture, and thrive readily on the poorer soils. Maize, millet, panic, and other small grains which are chiefly the food of the poorer classes, are very generally sown, especially in the hilly regions and western districts; and there is an extensive culture of mustard, sesamum, linseed, and Palma Christi, to supply the vast consumption of oil by the natives of the country. The plough of B. is drawn by a single yoke of oxen guided by the ploughman himself; but three pair of oxen are assigned to every plough, and these relieve each other till the daily task be completed. Several ploughs in succession deepen the same furrows, or rather scratch the surface, as it has no contrivance for turning the earth, and the share has neither width nor depth to stir a new soil; a second ploughing crosses the first; and a third is sometimes given diagonally to the preceding. These frequently repeated, and followed by the substitute for the harrow-which is generally nothing more than the branch of a tree-pulverize the surface, and prepare it for the seed. For the extirpation of weeds after the crops have risen above the ground, the labourers employ a short-handed spade, and place themselves at their work in a sitting posture. There are two seasons of reaping in the year; one in April, called the little harvest, which consists of the smaller grains; and another called the grand harvest, which is wholly of rice, though in some places three crops of this grain are raised in one year. But nothing can be conceived more tedious and injudicious than the mode of reaping. A mixture of different crops is frequently sown together in the same field; and, as these ripen in succession, the husbandman must either gather them singly—which occasions great destruction to the later plants by their being repeatedly trodden down-or must wait till he can reap the whole at once, which causes an equal loss in the more early grains by over ripeness. The corn of every description, after being reaped, is carelessly piled up without any defence from the weather, to be trodden out by the cattle, or thrashed by the staff of the husbandman at his convenience; and the grain, after being winnowed in the wind, is stored in jars of unbaked earth, and baskets made of twigs or grass, or is hoarded above ground in round huts, the floor of which, on account of the dampness of the climate and the moisture of the soil, is raised a foot or two above the surface. With an excellent soil and climate, and almost every variety of cultivated grains, and with a competent number of labourers, commanded at a small expense, the imperfection of B. husbandry is nevertheless great beyond what might have been expected. Sufficient attention is not paid to the proper periods of sowing; little care is employed in selecting the best varieties of each kind of grain; the most valuable crops are not steadily preferred in cultivation; the implements, as we have said, are scanty and incompetent; and the rotation of crops is not understood. The dung of the cattle is dried for fuel; and, except in cultivating the sugar-cane, mulberry, tobacco, and poppy, no manure is applied; neither is the land duly fallowed and cleaned. Drill husbandry, though known in the more remote districts of Hindostan, is not practised in B., even in the culture of the sugar-cane. There are few enclosures, and the roads are not kept in proper repair. Even the genial nature of the climate, and remarkable fertility of the soil, have contributed to prevent that exertion of ingenuity and application of labour which, in more barren regions, and under more unpropitious skies, have brought the cultivation of the soil to the highest state of improvement. All these circumstances have operated so strongly as obstacles to the progress of agriculture in B., that this most useful and first practised of human arts must be considered either as being here still in its infancy, or as having greatly degenerated. The free resort of Englishmen to India as settlers may be expected to produce most beneficial results on agricultural industry and the arts generally by the introduction of British skill, capital, and enterprise. This has been made sufficiently apparent in the indigo cultivation. So long as native growers and manufacturers only were employed in the production of this valuable article, the indigo of India was not of quality sufficient to compete with the produce of the West; but no sooner had Europeans embarked in the cultivation of this article in India than the Spanish-American cultivators were outstripped in the market; and the factories of B. now supply nearly four-fifths of the entire consumption of this article.—The monopoly of opium in B. supplies the government with a revenue of from £700,000 to £1,000,000 per ann. The duty imposed amounts to <math>301\frac{3}{4}</math> per cent. on the cwt. of the article. It is chiefly grown in the districts of Sarun and Tirpoets in Behar, and in the prov. of Benares. There is one circumstance, however, peculiar to the lower districts of this province, which frequently renders abortive the utmost skill and diligence of the husbandman, viz., an excess or a deficiency in the annual inundations. When their increase is gradual, the growth of the rice keeps pace with the rise of the waters, is thus always above its surface, and is frequently reaped in boats; but when the inundations rise too rapidly, or much above their ordinary level, the rice is overtopped and destroyed by the flood. The immense and expensive dykes already mentioned, and which are intended to guard against such disastrous occurrences, are frequently found to be very feeble barriers against the gathering stream of the Ganges. Its huge volume of water, bursting through the strongest, or rising over the highest, of its banks, will sometimes spread far and wide over the level plains, sweep away every thing in its course, and cover the richest fields with a bed of barren sand. The fatal effects of a dry season are still more extensive and destructive; and in order to provide some source of relief under the pressure of such a calamity, numerous reservoirs, called tanks, of an oblong square shape, and frequently more than an acre in extent, are constructed throughout the country. These being filled with water in the rainy season, afford the inhabitants, during the dry period, a supply of water for domestic uses, superior in quality to that of the Ganges, and, by means of irrigation, form a grand instrument of fertilizing the parched fields. In the higher parts of B., this practice is, at all seasons, an indispensable requisite in husbandry; and as it is there always necessary, it is more effectually and industriously administered. "Towards the end of the rains, the fields are well-ploughed in the ordinary manner; but, before sowing the seeds, they are divided into little square plots resembling the chequers of a backgammon table. Each square is surrounded with a shelving border, about four inches high, capable of containing water. Between the square chequers, thus constructed, small dykes are formed for conveying a rivulet over the whole field. As soon as the water has stood a sufficient time in one square to imbibe moisture, it is let off into the adjoining one, by opening a small outlet through the surrounding dyke. Thus one square after another is saturated, till the whole field, of whatever extent, is gone over." [Tennant's Indian Recreations, vol. ii. p. 167.] In the flat countries, however, these means of remedying the occasional deficiencies in the fall of the rains, and the flow of the rivers, are not provided with sufficient care; and the reservoirs, water-courses, &c., are more generally in a progress of decay than of improvement. But no possible precautions can prevent the failure of the crops in unfavourable seasons; and as, in consequence of the feeble and scanty husbandry practised in India, there is very rarely, even in plentiful years, any surplus produce to guard against the effects of a scarcity the inhabitants of a country which is, perhaps, the most fertile in the world, are more frequently than any other people the victims of absolute want. Even the prov. of B., the most fruitful in India, was often visited in former times with the calamity of a famine; of which a very dreadful instance occurred in the year 1770, in consequence of an unusual drought. When the granaries of the nabobs and of the company began to fail, and rice could no longer be supplied to the poorer classes, thousands expired of hunger in the fields and in the streets of Calcutta. Their dead bodies, mangled by dogs and vultures, tainted the air, and threatened a pestilence in addition to the miseries of famine. About 100 persons were daily employed by the company in throwing the dead bodies into the river, -a process which corrupted the water, and rendered the fish unwholesome nourishment. The hogs, ducks, and geese, also fed so much upon human carcases, that the only animal food which could be used was mutton; while that, on account of the dry season, was so extremely small, that a quarter of a sheep scarcely weighed a pound and a half. Mr. Everest, from an examination of the returns of prices of grain at Chinsurah, has found that the principal periods of scarcity were in 1752, 1770, 1788, 1807, and 1826, or at an average interval of 181 years. Vegetable productions.] The most important of the vegetable productions of B., besides pulse and grain, are tobacco, indigo, cotton, the mulberry, poppy, guavas, plantains, pomelos, limes, oranges, pomegranates, melons, pine-apples, the banyan tree, the pisang or banana; the cocoa-nut palm, which supplies a cordage called coir; the sugar-cane, which thrives in every district, and might be still more successfully cultivated in all; the betel-vine, a species of pepper, raised in almost every village; the mangotree, the fruit of which is in the highest estimation, and is almost universally used during the hot months; the date-tree, which grows everywhere, and which yields a sweet liquid of an intoxicating quality, from which sugar is sometimes extracted; the suri tree, which also affords, by incision of the stem, a clear, sweet, inebriating juice, which when sour is sometimes used instead of vinegar; the areca, the wood of which is as tough as whalebone, and its nut a useful article of food; and the bassia, abounding in the hilly countries and poorer soils, the flowers of which are esculent and nutritious, while its oil is a frequent substitute for butter. In the gardens are cultivated most of the vegetables of other climates fit for culinary purposes. The potato, particularly, has been introduced with considerable success, and thrives best in the dry seasons which are destructive to the ricecrops. The various sorts of flowering trees and shrubs, which either grow wild, or thrive with little care, are too numerous to be mentioned in this place; but we may notice as among the most remarkable and beautiful, the chulta, the flower of which is at first a hard green ball, on foot-stalks about four inches in length. The calyx, after the ball has opened, is composed of five round, thick, succulent leaves, and the corolla of the same number of fine white petals. After continuing only one day, the corolla drops, and the ball closes; while a succession of these opening and shutting flowers continues during the space of several months. A tall tree called the tatoon, has leaves of a deep shining green colour, and fruit resembling an olive, with a kernel like the date. A large spreading tree called russa, has a peculiar rich and beautiful appearance when in full bloom, as it is then covered with flowers of a bright crimson, or of a bright yellow, or of some intervening shade between these two colours. Of this remarkable tree, however, it is said, that there are only two plants known in B.; one of which is in the neighbourhood of Calcutta, and the other near the Dutch settlements. It has been mentioned, as one cause of its scarcity, that the ants and other insects are so fond of its seed that not one pod can be found entire and uninjured. Animal kingdom.] Wild boars, bears, wolves, foxes, jackalls, hyænas, leopards, panthers, lynxes, hares, deer, zebras, wild buffaloes, antelopes, apes and monkeys, elephants, tigers, are natives of B. The foxes are feeble and slender; the hare and deer are inferior to those of Europe, and the venison in general lean and insipid. The dogs are generally of the cur species, with sharp erect ears and pointed tails. There is found in the eastern districts an undescribed animal called the gyal, which may be placed between the domestic bull and the buffalo. The rhinoceros with one horn, abounds in the isles of the Ganges. But the royal tiger of B. is most of all worthy of particular notice. It seems to have been known to the Romans, and is distinguished by Seneca by the appellation of Gangentica tigris. This animal is sometimes five or six feet in height, and of such enormous strength as to carry off a large bullock. The horses, chiefly used in B. by the grandees and Europeans, are of the Persian or Arabian breed. The native horses are thin, ugly, and ill-shaped animals, tolerably active, but, in their best state, not equal to the Welsh and Highland ponies. B. is more defective in its breed of cattle than most other parts of India. The pastures are overstocked; and the black cattle and hogs are barely kept alive: herds of the former may often be seen in such a starved state, that not one of them would weigh against a good English sheep. Goats and sheep thrive better; but the latter are small, of a lank figure, and black or dark grey colour, with coarse, thin, and hairy wool.—Game, poultry, and water fowl of all descriptions are found in the greatest abundance in B. Ducks are numerous; and the common domestic fowl of Europe run wild in the woods, and are known as the jungle fowl. In consequence of the humanity of the natives towards the lower animals, the crow, kite, mino, and sparrow, hop about the dwellings with entire familiarity, and, even in the houses of the English, will pilfer from the dishes of meat as they are carried from the cooking-room to the hall. Among the feathered tribes may be particularly mentioned the argill or hurgill, (Ardea argala), a species of heron, which is very large and ravenous, and held in great veneration by the Bramins. It is ludicrously termed the adjutant, from his erect posture and military strut, stalks at his ease at the side of the natives, and devours abundance of toads, lizards, serpents, and insects. Minerals.] The mineral productions of this prov. are unimportant. Iron ore is found in the hills of Silhet, and coal is wrought in the Jungle Mahals. Coal and iron are likewise wrought in Birbhum. Inland commerce. The inland external commerce of B. is carried on chiefly with Tibet, Agra, and Delhi. The principal articles of this trade are silks, calicoes, muslins, saltpetre, opium, sugar, indigo, gum lac, and a variety of piece-goods. The exportation of grain from the corn-countries, and the importation of salt, constitute the greater part of this trade, which is in the hands of the natives. The importation of cotton from the western provinces, and the exchange of tobacco and betel-nut, form almost the whole internal commerce. Some part of this merchandise is transported by land-carriage; which is commonly performed by oxen, sometimes by the small horses of the country, and rarely by buffaloes, because though these animals are both stronger and more docile than oxen, they are less easily maintained. The magnificent causeways, formerly constructed by the native princes, are now fallen into decay; and the country affords no substantial materials for the formation of highways; so that, except in the neighbourhood of military stations, there are no roads sufficient to admit the use of wheel-carriages. These disadvantages, however, are to a certain extent compensated for by the facility with which internal navigation is conducted. So completely are the various branches of the Ganges and the Brahmaputra diffused over the flat country, that scarcely any part of this large province is above 25 m. distant from a navigable river, or natural canal. "These rivers are in a state of tranquillity, from the time of the change of the monsoon in October till the middle of March, when the north-westers begin in the eastern parts of B., and may be expected once in three or four days, until the commencement of the rainy season. These north-westers are the most formidable enemies that are met with in the inland navigation, being sudden and violent squalls of wind and rain; and, though of no long duration, are often attended with fatal effects if not carefully guarded against, -whole fleets of trading boats having been sunk by them almost instantaneously. They are more frequent in the eastern, than in the western part of B.; and happen oftener towards the close of the day, than at any other time. As they are indicated some hours before they arrive, by the rising and very singular appearance of the clouds, the traveller has commonly time enough to seek a place of shelter. It is in the great rivers alone, that they are so truly formidable; and that about the latter end of May and the beginning of June, when the rivers are much increased in width." "During the long interval between the end of the rainy season and the beginning of the north-westers, one proceeds in security with respect to weather; and has only to observe a common degree of attention to the piloting the boat clear of shallows and stumps of trees." "From the beginning of November to the latter end of May, the usual rate of going with the stream is 40 m. in a day of 12 hours; and during the rest of the year from 50 to 70 m.: the current is strongest while the waters of the inundation are draining-off; which happens in part of August and September." "Seventeen or twenty miles a-day, according to the ground, and the number of impediments, is the greatest distance that a large budgerow can be towed against the stream during the fair season; and to accomplish this, the boat must be drawn at the rate of 1½ m. per hour, through the water, for twelve hours." [Rennell.] The vessels employed in this navigation are variously constructed, according to the nature of the rivers on which they are employed; and are of various sizes, from 8 to 24 oars. Some have cabins 14 ft. wide, and proportionably long; and draw from 4 to 5 ft. water. The larger boats upon the Ganges carry from 300 to 600 mauns. In one navigation, wherein the vessels descend with the stream and return with the track-rope, their construction consults neither aptitude for the sail nor the oar; in others, where the passage is assisted by the stream of one river, and opposed by the current of the next, the chief dependence is on the oar, for the winding narrow passage allows no use of the sail, and, as the shallows are frequent, the Bengalese vessels admit of no keels. These vessels are very cheaply constructed. A circular board tied to a bamboo forms the oar; a wooden triangular frame loaded with some weighty substance, the anchor; a few bamboos lashed together supply the mast; a cane of the same species serves as a yard for the sail, which is made of coarse sackcloth; the trees of the country afford resin to pitch the vessel; and a platform of mats thatched with straw supplies the place of a deck | to shelter the merchandise. They are navigated, too, with equal frugality; the boatmen receive little more than their food, which is most commonly furnished in grain, together with an inconsiderable allowance of money for the purchase of salt, and the supply of other petty wants. In this trade it is calculated that upwards of 300,000 boatmen are employed, the greater part of whom are at the same time partly agriculturists or fishermen. A great part of the inland navigation of this country, however, is now performed by means of steamers, which keep up a constant communication between Calcutta, Patna, Benares, Mirzapore, Allahabad, and the other great cities on the Ganges, at an average speed of 50 m. per diem, which speed will probably soon be doubled. Roads.] The Grand trunk-road from Calcutta, the main artery of communication throughout B. and Hindostan, extends to a length of 770 m., with a general breadth of 30 ft., increased in some places to 40 ft.—The road from Puri to Bissenpore, which connects Orissa with B., and which is commonly known as the Jugurmath-road, is held to be of the next importance.—A road from Silhet to Gow hatty, the capital of Assam, across the Cassia hills, is in active preparation. The Deccan road from Mirzapore to Jubbulpore, a distance of 239 m., commenced in 1824, has been completed lately. -Another road, small in point of expense, but of great importance, has been made from the eastern frontier of B., through Cachar, and across the Munipore hills to the limits of the Burmese empire. Besides these roads, which are stated to be the most prominent, a variety of district-roads have added greatly to the local convenience of the people, and have proportionately occupied attention. A toll on a road is unknown. Canals.] The canals which fringe the eastern part of the city of Calcutta, connected with the Isamutte river, are of the highest importance to the welfare of the city, as the produce of all the eastern districts is thus brought to it with little or no risk. The canals are very productive of revenue, for the tolls on those in the vicinity of Calcutta are said to yield on an average 1,21,800 rupees a-year, while the annual average charge for their maintenance appears to be about 45,000 rupees.—The Ganges canal. as first projected by Captain Cantley, was only to pass from Hurdwar to Mynpuri, and to be restricted to purposes of irrigation, but it was extended in 1842 to Cawnpore and Allahabad, a direct length of 450 m., and a total length, including its branches, of 878 m., when it was also resolved that it should be adapted, as far as possible, for navigation, and, in fact, that this should, if practicable, be the leading consideration. Captain Cantley, however, after the requisite surveys, still held to the opinion that irrigation should be the grand object; and, in consequence, the original plan is to be so far maintained that the superfluous slope only is to be disposed of in descents, with lockage, while the main body of water is to constitute an artificial river. "I consider," he says, "that all the available water in the Doab properly belongs to the agriculturist and the soil. The severe droughts which occur in the north-west provinces can hardly be appreciated by those who have been accustomed to climates liable to a greater degree of moisture, or whose observations on Indian agriculture are confined to B. The time, moreover, is approaching when railroads will necessarily supersede the use of canals for navigation." The first estimate for this work was about £600,000; and it was commenced in 1842, the annual expenditure being limited to £20,000. Subsequently, however, the estimate for the entire work has been raised to £1,000,000. Some portions of this magnificent work will present architectural features of an important kind, amongst which is an aqueduct of 15 arches over the Solani river; and it is also proposed that plantations of forest trees should be established on each side of the canal, and, at distances of 15 m., orchards of grafted mango trees -a fruit highly prized by the native community,-each orchard to consist of five acres. These are to be annually farmed out, and "as each orchard will be the nucleus of distribution for grafts when the parent-trees have arrived at maturity, it may be inferred that a very superior fruit will be generally disseminated." When this canal is completed it is presumed it will be used for the whole or the greater part of the hill-produce, which is now rafted down the Garges, the main articles being timber of different sizes, bamboos, firewood, charcoal, and the different grasses used either in forming rafts, or those which sell in the market for rope and other purposes. Railroads.] The mode of communication by railways must ere long be introduced into this splendid country; and the advantages, in a military as well as commercial point of view, which must arise from connecting the different main points of our great Indian empire by means of railways, are too obvious to require illustration at our hands. Among the projected lines, the most important perhaps, is what is called the East Indian. This great trunk line, which is destined ultimately to connect the seat of the supreme government with its most distant provinces, will, it is understood, commence at or near Calcutta-most probably at Howrah, on the opposite bank of the Hooghly-and proceed by Burdwan, through the hilly country, and crossing the Soane river, terminate, for the present, at Mirzapore. So far as Burdwan is concerned, Mr. Andrews, in his valuable pamphlet on Indian railways, expresses his opinion that the project is feasible enough, MARROW S S S S S S S S S S S S S S S S S S S this much of the line being easy in construction, and the mineral and other traffic holding out a remunerative prospect; but as the proposed line from Calcutta to Rajmahl can supply the requisite accommodation to the collieries of Burdwan, he thinks that the East Indian, or, as it is called in India, "the political line, should, for various reasons, devote its energies elsewhere, as the best mode of fulfilling its ambitous hopes of being, as the Ganges is now, the great highway of India, and break ground at Mirzapore, instead of Calcutta, and crossing the Jumna, proceed by Cawnpore to Agra and Delhi." A direct line from Calcutta to Mirzapore must have difficulties to encounter amongst the hills, to say nothing of the Herculean labour of crossing the Soane; there are no considerable towns or stations to accommodate; the pop. is scattered, it being estimated that, in the route for a distance of 200 m., the pop. does not exceed one inhabitant per mile, and the country generally but little productive; and although it should have, as its upper terminus, Mirzapore, the great central commercial mart of India, Mr. A does not think the through traffic, on which it must be dependent, would answer the expectations of the promoters, as it would be in direct competition not only with the usual river craft on the Ganges-which has ever been the great commercial artery of the country-but the several powerful and influential companies for the inland steam navigation, intimately connected as they are with the principal merchants and capitalists, would be most formidable rivals, Calcutta, Patna, Benares, Mirzapore, Allahabad, and the other great cities and towns on the river, proceeding at the average speed of 50 miles per diem; and this rate of progression, an eminent merchant informed us the other day, would soon be doubled, from their steamers being enabled to continue their course by night as well as by day. Thus we should have a line not free from difficulty, a poor part of a rich country, and a formidable opposition, without advancing the seat of government to any great extent towards the frontier. Steamers have gone for many years to Allahabad, 43 miles higher up than Mirzapore. Another objection to the selection of this as an experimental or preliminary portion of the trunk line is, that terminating at Mirzapore or Allahabad, it leaves the traveller or goods at the very point where the real difficulty begins,—the Ganges, above Allahabad, from its ledges and shallows being closed to steamers, and, excepting for a few months in the year, the navigation even for country craft is very precarious, and the Jumna, from the sharp and angular rocks which rise from its bed, is still more dangerous and difficult. The projectors of the Great North of India line, knowing that the communication between Calcutta and Allahabad was already provided for by the inland steam navigation companies, commence their proposed line at Allahabad, where the actual difficulties in the river transit begin. This line will form a great trunk-line between that city, Cawnpore, Agra, and Delhi; "but as the completion of comparatively short lines is the best mode of introducing railways into any country," it is proposed to complete this undertaking section by section. Should this plan be adhered to, Mr. Andrews recommends as a first step the connecting of any two points between Cawnpore and Delhi-for example, a line from Cawnpore via Etawah, or Mynpuri, to Agra, or from Agra by Muttra and Allighur to Delhi; the traffic on the road above Cawnpore being much greater than below that station, and the ground being, if anything, still better adapted for a railway. The Great North of India proposes a continuation of the line from Allahabad to Mirzapore, and from Delhi to the NW frontier. The next design which merits attentive consideration, from the immediate facilities which it would give to government and commerce, is the line from Calcutta to Rajmahl. This railway was ushered into life under the auspices of a company denominated the Great Western Railway of Bengal; and from the capitalists and merchants of Calcutta extending to it their patronage and and densely-peopled portion of our dominions, it will form the Another projected line is the Great Indian Peninsula line, the ENE of Bombay—close to Kampti, the most central military position in India-it will proceed either to Calcutta, or, what is more probable, join the great East India trunk-line at Mirzapore, which is 890 m. ENE of Bombay. This latter arrangement would give us our intelligence from Upper India and Bengal a week earlier than at present; and, in conjunction with the trunk-line from Calcutta to Delhi, would enable the combined armies of the two presidencies to concentrate their powers on almost any point of our dominions that could by possibility be threatened. This enterprise will, for the present, curb its ambition, till it has solved the difficult problem of climbing the Ghauts." Maritime commerce.] The maritime trade of B., as far as it is managed by the natives, was never so extensive as the inland. The principal part of it is conveyed by way of Calcutta. Vessels of moderate burden occasionally load with rice in some of the Sunderbund branches, and large vessels are sometimes built at Islamabad; still Calcutta is the only port of any importance in the province. A considerable part of the trade between India and China is carried on from Calcutta; and the most valuable part of this trade consists in the shipment of opium. In 1820-21, 5,147 chests of opium were shipped at Calcutta for countries beyond the three Residencies, and of this quantity 3,591 chests went to China. In 1829-30, the quantity shipped was 9,678 chests, of which 7,443 went to China. In 1839-40, the quantity amounted to 18,510 chests, of which 3,755 chests went to China, and 13,676 to Penang and eastward. In 1841-2, the export amounted to 19,739 chests, of which China received 10,752, and Penang 8,761. The returns from China are made partly in bullion, partly in the Company's investments, and partly in goods sent to India, especially to the Bombay side. B. also ships to China, rice, saltpetre, woollen and cotton goods, coral, and pearls. Piece-goods, rice, and opium, are extensively exported to Singapore, which forms the entrepot of the trade between B. and the straits of Malacca, Borneo, and the Moluccas. A traffic in rice, cottons, and silks, is carried on with the Maldives in exchange for cowries; and with Assam by supplying it with quantities of salt, receiving in payment gold, silver, ivory, gum lac, and silk. Rice is exported to Ceylon, opium to Java, cotton to Malabar, and silk to Surat. Rice, gum lac, and cottons, are sent to Bassora, receiving in exchange dried fruits, rose water, and gold; rice and sugar are sent to the coast of Coromandel, and generally paid for with specie or chunk-shells; and a variety of rich merchandise is exported to Arabia, which returns bullion, dates, raisins, coral, and pearls. These branches of trade, though passing chiefly through the hands of Europeans, and carried on under their protection, are not always solely on their account, but frequently in conjunction with Gentoo, and especially Armenian merchants, great numbers of whom, since the revolutions in Persia, have settled upon the banks of the Ganges. To England, B. exports indigo to an amount nearly equal to one-half its total exports to Europe. Raw silk and saltpetre form the next articles in importance. France likewise receives a large amount of saltpetre and indigo. The United States take silk, piece-goods and indigo. The exports from Calcutta in 1841-2 were as follows: Countries to which exported.
