Manufactures.] The principal manufactures and articles of trade which B. could furnish to the merchant in great abundance and perfection, are cotton piece-goods of various descriptions; calicoes, a name applied to several kinds of cloth, to which no English mames have yet been affixed; pack-thread, woven into sack-cloth, and employed as clothing by the mountaineers; cotton canvass, flannel, and blankets; dimities of various kinds and patterns, and cloths resembling diaper and damask linen; wove silk taffeta, plain and flowered; tissues, brocades, plain and ornamented gauzes; a mixed cloth of silk and cotton; filature silk, and tessa or wild silk; grain, sugar, tobacco, indigo, saltpetre, hides, gums, liquorice, ginger, and a great variety of medicinal and dyeing The zemindary of Benares, the city of Patna, drugs. and the vicinity of Calcutta, are the principal seats of the manufacture of chintzes, which appears to be an original art in India. The articles which are most in demand in B., are japan, copper, tin, lead, and a variety of European commodities. Mr. Chappermit, are maintained by established annual contributions, chiefly paid in grain, with little reference to the quantity or quality of their work. Like the rest of the system to which it belongs, this arrangement was evidently devised with a view to securing to each village what it needed for its own use, without the employment of money, or dependence on other disdency to reform all this, and none other could do it so effectually as a railway. By establishing workshops at many points, by occasioning a demand for highlyskilled labour, and by bringing within easy reach of every village the productions of superior workmen, wherever situated, it would raise interests and wishes Population.] From the want of public registers, the amount of the pop. in the provinces of India has never been very exactly ascertained; but various calsoil, and the slender vegetable diet required by the population, about four-fifths consist of native Hindus, and the remainder of Moguls. Ram Comulsen emigrated to B. from the provinces of Upper Hindostan, the original inhabitants retiring before them into the countries to the E and the W of Bengal, while some crossed the Brahmaputra, and settled in the regions beyond it. The Moguls are the of B. they are nearly as numerous as the Hindus. They are of an olive colour, with features resembling the European. They are all Mahommedans; and hold the idolatry of the Hindus in great abhorrence. The Hindus are slender, handsome, and well made, resembling Europeans in stature; of a dark brown colour, and sometimes a yellowish complexion, with hair black and uncurled. Most of them shave their heads, eradicate the hair from every part of their bodies, and go almost naked, with only a piece of linen round their middle; but those of a higher rank are accustomed to wear turbans, and a dress of white cotton, which reaches from the shoulders to the feet. The dress of the women consists of drawers, a loose coat, and a piece of cotton cloth thrown over the shoulders; their head is uncovered, and their hair fastened up behind. They are fond of loading every part of their body, their hair, arms, legs, fingers, toes, and even nostrils, with all kinds of ornaments. Bengali language.] Upwards of 20,000,000 of the pop. of the B. provinces speak a dialect holding the same relation to the Sanscrit that the modern Italian or Spanish does to the Latin, with a small infusion of words derived from other sources. Into this language the Serampore missionaries have translated the Scriptures, and several religious and elementary works; and a dictionary of the language, founded on Todd's edition of Johnson's Dictionary, was published at Calcutta in 1834 by Ram Comulsen. Dr. Carey also published a dictionary in Bengali and English, in 3 vols. 4to., in 1825. Several other dictionaries of this language exist; and grammars have been published by Halhed in 1778; Carey in 1805; Houghton in 1821; Keith in 1834; and Rammohun Roy in 1833. Owing to the abundance of Sanscrit works, and the Brahminical prejudice against the Bengali, no books or pamphlets were ever published in it until the establishment of the college of Fort William. European settlements.] Four different European nations have formed establishments in B. for commercial purposes, viz., the English, French, Dutch, and Danes. The Danish settlement of Serampore-now given up to Great Britain-extends about 2 m. on the eastern banks of the Ganges, and is of very inconsiderable breadth. It is completely surrounded by the British dominions; has no fortifications except a small battery for saluting; and, on the dispute with the Northern powers, a party of Sepoys took possession of it without the least resistance. The French settlement of Chandernagore, and the Dutch one of Chinsurah, were more extensive than the Danish; but, from the larger establishments kept up, have always cost more than they produced. When the East India company appropriated to themselves the opium and saltpetre trade, to prevent any competition in the market, they agreed to allow a certain quantity annually to the Danes, French, and Dutch, at a specific price, on condition that they should not purchase any from the natives. These treaties, however, were not renewed with the French and Dutch, who considered the surrender of their settlements, without that advantage, as a very useless gift. Chinsurah has since been given up by the Dutch. The English established a commercial intercourse with B. at a very early period; and made a settlement on the Ganges in the beginning of the 17th cent. Their first factory was at the town of Hugli; but in 1689 it was removed to Calcutta, which is about 26 m. farther down the river. A regiment of regular infantry was applied for by the united East India company, in 1684; and this was the first disembarcation of British soldiers in India. By means of their fort and garrison, they protected, from the demands of the rajas, their trading-vessels, which came down from Patna; but in the beginning of the 18th cent., they obtained from Feroksere, great grandson of Aurungzebe, a firman or grant, exempting their trade from all duties; and, while the company stood in need of protection against the native princes, this was regarded as their commercial charter in India. From the year 1742, they had frequent occasion to resist, by force of arms, the attacks of the Mahratta states, and of the nabobs of B, till the famous battle of Plassey, in 1757, laid the foundation of their power in that country. From that period they became the arbiters of the succession to the nabobship; and in 1765 assumed the government of that prov., receiving from the nominal Mogul, Shah Aulem, a grant of the revenues of B., Bahar, and Orissa, on condition of paying 26 lacs of rupees (about £260,000) per annum. Since this last date, B., with its dependencies, have continued, BEN without interruption, under the power of Great Britain. "If Madras was the cradle of the English power, Calcutta was its nursery where it grew to strength and maturity." And, whatever diversity of opinion may exist, respecting the means by which that power was established, there can be no question, that it has proved a most beneficial exchange to the natives. In all these provinces, the limits of order have uniformly extended with the progress of European dominion; for the space of half-a-century the calamities of war and of military depredation have been removed from their habitations; and they have enjoyed a degree of security in their persons and properties, at present unknown in any other part of Asia. For other particulars, respecting the civil history of B., its commercial connections with Great Britain, its government and revenues, the religion, language, manners and customs of its inhabitants, we refer our readers to the articles India, Hindostan, Bombay, CALCUTTA, DACCA, GANGES, HINDUS, SIKHS, BRAMINS, &c.—Rennell's Memoir of a Map of Hindostan.— Tennant's Indian Recreations.—Asiatic Researches and Journal.—Mill's History of British India.—Parliamentary Reports.

BENGAL (BAY OF), a large triangular gulf of the Indian ocean, comprehended within the parallels of 8° and 20° N lat., and the meridians of 80° and 92° E long. The coast of the prov. of Bengal forms its western side; on the east it has the coasts of Arracan, Pegu, and the Malayan peninsula. Between Chittagong on the E, and Balasore on the W, at the head of the gulf, its breadth is about 250 m. in a direct line; between the Malayan peninsula and Cape Camorin, a line drawn across its mouth will measure 1,200 m. Some hydrographers, however, take the southern limits of the gulf between the S point of Ceylon and the NW point of Sumatra. There are no good harbours on its western coast, and no soundings at the distance of 30 m. from the beach; but the eastern coast presents several good harbours, and soundings may be obtained within 2 m, of the coast. The SW monsoon begins to blow on the Coromandel, or western coast of the gulf, in the latter end of March, or the beginning of April; but does not attain its full strength and regularity till June, land and sea-breezes being not uncommon in March, April, and May. Even during the months of June, July, and August, a land-breeze often blows from shore for 24 or 48 hours. In September, the SW monsoon declines; and the NE monsoon commences about the middle of October, from which time, till the beginning of December, the navigation of the gulf is dangerous. During the months of December, January, and February, the weather is generally settled and calm. In the NE monsoon, the current sets at times strong to the southward; during the SW monsoon, it runs along the Coromandel coast to the northward. During both monsoons, a heavy surf rolls in along the whole Coromandel coast. The access to the Hugli, in both monsoons, is very difficult,

BENGAL (Presidency of), the most extensive and important of the three presidencies into which the British possessions in India are divided for the purposes of administration. In Hindostan, or to the W of the Ganges, it comprises nearly 310,000 sq. m.; while beyond the Ganges, it embraces nearly 50,000 sq. m. The nucleus of this immense territory was the settlement of Fort William, now Calcutta, and a small adjacent territory which in 1707 was dignified with the title of a presidency. In 1757, the nabob of B. ceded the districts known as the Twenty-four pergunnahs. In 1765 the presidency, by the cession of the whole province of Bengal, and part of Bahar and of Orissa, was enlarged in territorial extent to upwards of 150,000 sq. m. In 1775 the rich zemindary of Benares, containing 12,000 sq. m., was added to the presidency. These acquisitions were enlarged in 1801 by the cession of upwards of 32,000 sq. m. by the nabob of Oude; and in 1803 a further cession was obtained from Sindia of at least an equal amount of territory. In 1819, on the final destruction of the Mahratta confederacy, another vast addition was made to the Upper or North-west provinces of this presidency; and in 1826 the rajah of Berar ceded 55,900 sq. m.; while the conquest of the Birmese provinces, in the same year, added 50,000 sq. m. to this presidency. The lower provinces of the B. presidency are divided into the four collectorates of Calcutta, Patna, Murshedabad, and Dacca, having a total area of 153,802 sq. m. The Upper or Western provinces form the collectorates of Benares and Bareilly, with an area of 66,510 sq. m. The ceded districts on the Nerbudda, and those obtained from the rajah of Berar in 1826, amount to 85,700 sq. m. Total 306,012 sq. m., being the area of the presidency on this side of the Ganges. To which falls to be added the area of the Trans-Gangetic provinces, amounting to 52,757 sq. m. Total 358,769 sq. m. The pop. of the Upper and Lower provinces in 1822 was estimated at 69,710,071; but to this must be added that of the districts ceded in 1826, on both sides of the Ganges, amounting it is probable to 20,000,000; but of which no returns exist. Government.] The government of this immense and populous territory is vested in the governor-general of India, whose seat of government is Calcutta, and who has a supreme controlling power over the governors of the other two presidencies of Madras and Bombay. He is assisted by five councillors, three of whom are appointed by the East India company; the fourth likewise is appointed by them, but subject to the approbation of the sovereign; and the fifth is the commander-in-chief for the time, who takes precedence after the governor-general. The system of local government which obtains in India will be found detailed in our article HINDOSTAN. Revenue. The gross revenue of the B. presidency and its subordinate settlements, during the 15 years ending in 1828-9 was £196,121,983; and the territorial charges, exclusive of those paid in England, during these 15 years, amounted to £167,747,449; showing a large surplus revenue amounting to an annual average of £1,891,635; while the accounts of the other two presidencies exhibited a deficit. The net revenue and expenditure of the presidency, including the prov. of Scinde, in each of the years ending 30th April, from 1837 to 1845, were as follows: Expenditure. Net revenue. The allowances and charges of collection on the gross revenue in each of the above years averaged £2,500,000; and the home-charges on the total Indian revenue, averaged nearly the same. The Bengal debt amounted on 30th April 1845 to 38,92,84,104 rupees, bearing an annual interest of 1,82,35,842 rupees. Besides a debt of 2,00,000 rupees in the north-west provinces. Army. The B. army numbers nearly 100,000 effective soldiers, commanded by about 2,400 European officers. It is considered to possess the highest caste men, being largely composed of Rajputs. The Hindus are in the full proportion of threefourths to the Mahommedans in the native infantry of B. The latter are chiefly troopers. The Rajputs in the army of B. amount probably to 40,000. The great proportion of the Rajput grenadiers are 6 ft. and upwards; and the standard below which no recruit is taken is 5 ft. 6 in. The entire B. establishment forms 99 corps, viz., 3 of horse artillery; 7 of foot artillery; a corps of engineers, equal to three others in the strength of its officers; 10 of native cavalry; 2 of European infantry; and 74 of native infantry. Each corps is commanded by a colonel, lieutenant-colonel, major, 5 captains, 8 lieutenants, L BEN and 4 second lieutenants.—There are 16 military stations in B., each under a general of brigade.
BENGAL PASSAGE, a channel of the Indian ocean which runs between the NW extremity of the island of Sumatra and the adjacent group of islands. It averages 12 m. in breadth.

BENGA'ZI, a town of Tripoli, in the district of Barca, on the E coast of the gulf of Sidra, 65 m. SW of Dolmeita, in N lat. 32° 7½', E long. 20° 2', at the extremity of a beautiful fertile plain, extending to the foot of a long chain of mountains about 14 m. distant to the SE. It is a poor and decaying place, with a pop. estimated by Della Cella in 1817 at 5,000; but Beechey was informed that it only contained about 2,000. Its port is fast filling up, and cannot now be entered by vessels drawing above 8 ft. of water. In rough weather large vessels avoid this part of the coast—which is rocky—and steer for the bay of Tajuni, 5 m. to the W. Its chief trade consists in the export of cattle to Malta. It also exports corn, wool, salt butter, and ostrich-feathers. The wool is sent to Tripoli. The export of ostrichfeathers is monopolised by the Jewish merchants, who pay a large tribute to the pasha for this privilege. The Beduins bring the skin of the ostrich, with all its feathers on, and receive for that of a male about 30 Spanish dollars; and for that of a female, 15. They are sent to Leghorn and Marseilles. Ships trading here find a plentiful supply of fresh provisions, with fruits, vegetables, and water. Captain Beechey spent the rainy season at B., which lasted from the middle of January to the beginning of March, and was accompanied with constant gales from the NE and NW-and found the streets during a part of the time literally converted into rivers, while the sheep and goats perished by thousands in the neighbourhood. The houses, being chiefly put together with mud, were continually crumbling to pieces under the torrents of rain; and when a house falls, it is generally left in a state of rubbish and ruin, so that the streets are blocked up by mounds of this nature which are heaps of mud and mire in winter, and in summer become so many depots of a canal which still affords a passage to small fishingboats. This may have been the Tritonis lacus of ancient geographers.
BENGEO, a parish in Hertfordshire, 1 m. NNE of Hertford. Area 3,020 acres. Pop. 1,141.
BENGERMOW, or Bangermow, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Oude, 50 m. W of Lucknow, and 17 m. SE of Canouge. It occupies an elevated site about 4 m. from the N bank of the Ganges.
BENGERWAD, or BENJAWAD, a town and ruined fortress of Tripoli, in the dist. of Sert, on the southern shore of the gulf of Sidra, 90 m. ESE of Taffran.
BENGKAHULU. See BENEVOLEN.

BENGO, a river of Lower Guinea, in the kingdom of Angola, which, after a course of about 195 m. from ENE to WSW, falls into the Atlantic at the bay of the same name, 9 m. N of the town of Angola, in S lat. 8° 50'.

BENGORE, a cape and a headland in co. Antrim, Ulster. The cape commences at the Giant's Causeway on the W, and sweeps round to Dunsaverick on the E; and contains, on its coast, many interesting objects generally noticed under the name of Giant's Causeway. This cape is the rival of Fair-head in wondrous attractions; and is similarly formed in its geognosy and colonnades. The headland of B., or 2 x that particular one of the series of promontories forming the cape which is par excellence 'Bengore' or 'the Goat's-Head,' rises 320 ft. from the edge and above the level of the sea; is situated a little to the E of Pleaskin; and, though similar to that columnar cliff in formation, is inferior to it in distinctness of stratification and in beauty.
BENGORKERA, or BUNGURKEERA, a small town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Berar, and dist. of Chuta Nagpur, 10 m. S of Deesah.
BENGOWER, a mountain in co. Galway, one of the loftiest and most conspicuous of the group of Binabola, or the Twelve Pins. It overhangs the lake of Ballinahinch. Alt. 2,336 ft.

BENGUELA, a country of W Africa; bounded on the N by Angola, from which it is separated by the Coanza river, in 9° 20' S lat.; and stretching southward along the coast to Cape Negro, in 15° 40' 7" S lat. Its eastern, or interior limits, are imperfectly known. The natives in the interior will not permit the Portuguese, or any other people with straight hair, to enter their territory [Owen]; but the country, it is reported, becomes very mountainous as the traveller advances from the coast; and lakes both of salt and of fresh water frequently occur. The general direction of the mountainranges is NNE and SSW, following the outline of the coast; and the larger rivers have a prevailing NW course. Captain Vidal, who surveyed the coast in conjunction with Captain Owen, in 1825-7, describes the coast immediately to the N of Cape Negro as arid, and presenting a very meagre vegetation. It does not appear that the Portuguese—the nominal masters of this coast—have any settlement to the S of Benguela, in 12° 12' S lat. But here the coast assumes an interesting appearance as beheld from the sea. Professor Smith, in his account of Tuckey's voyage, states that this part of the coast considerably resembles that of some of the Danish islands; and Dr. Tams says that, on approaching it, he could almost have fancied that the steep calcareous declivities of Moen, or of Stevenskhut, lay before him. "The mountain-slopes, which run down close to the sea, were covered to the summit with the most lovely verdure; and the intervening valleys were clad in smiling green." Mr. Maxwell, in his observations on Congo and Loango, describes the whole coast from the parallel of 3° 30' S to that of the town of New B., as magnificent. Perpendicular red cliffs, he says, in many places skirt the shore, while the back ground consists of mountains here receding far inland, there approaching the sea; the intermediate space between the ridges and the sea, being beautifully diversified with rising grounds and clumps of lofty trees. [Edinburgh Phil. Journ., Jan. 1822.] The principal rivers, proceeding northwards from Cape Negro, are the Rio dos Mortes, the Copororo, the Maribombo, the Catumbela, the Inhandanha, the Egito, the Quicunbo, the Gunza, the Cuvo, and the Longa. Besides these, several rivers do not appear to reach the coast; but to terminate in lagunes or marshes. - The coast is represented as peculiarly unhealthy during the rainy season, which lasts through May and June. "The climate affects even the natives: for during the prevalence of the short rains, they likewise suffer from intermittent fevers, which are succeeded by disorders of the spleen." [Tams.] During the day, the therm. generally rises in the shade to 93° or 94°, and sometimes to 102° or 105°. This intense heat compels all Europeans to retire for the day, and even the animal creation appears to shrink from it. From half-past 5 till 8 A. M. is the busiest part of the day.—The vegetation of this region of abundant moisture and heat is surpassingly BEN nanas, mangoes, and all the other fruits of the tropics are plentiful; besides every kind of European vegetable. The sugar-cane, manioc, and maize supply the principal articles of food. Cattle are scarce, but of a beautiful breed, with horns 4 and 5 ft. in length; hogs form the staple of animal food. Buffaloes, zebras, antelopes, and elephants are numerous in the forests, and all the three species of hyenas infest the environs of the towns and villages. A great variety of birds inhabit the forests. On the coast, fish and turtle are abundant; and alligators and hippopotami swarm in the rivers. When Captain Vidal visited the town of B., he was informed that some time previously, during a long drought, a number of elephants had actually invaded the town in a body in quest of water; and were not driven out until after a desperate conflict.—There is a sulphur-mine on the Rio Vittoria, a small creek near Cape Negro; and a gold-mine is said to exist about 100 m. in the interior.—Benguela is inhabited by a great number of small Negro tribes, who are distinguished from each other by their different styles of tattooing. Their clothing generally consists of a piece of blue calico, two or three yards in length, wrapped round the waist, and fastened with a string, or knotted together. Over this they wear the skin of an animal, generally of the tiger-cat, hanging down with the hairy side outwards. The richer Negro women add to the waist-cloth a large square garment, falling over the head and shoulders, and shrouding the entire figure, except the face. Strings of glass and porcelain beads are an indispensable ornament; and gold or brass ear-rings. The arms of the men are bows, javelins, lances, and clubs. Circumcision is almost general among the native tribes of this region; and they all burn their dead.—B. is attached, under the Portuguese regime, to the captain-generalship of Angola; and is divided into eight presidios: viz., Bailundo, Bihe, Caconda, Galange, Grande da Quinzamba, Haube, Quilengues e Sambos, and Quilengues e Huita. Its revenue in 1819 was returned at 37,287,160 reis; while that of Angola amounted to 175,202,419 reis. Benguela (Nova), the cap. of the above country, is situated in S lat. 12° 33′ 54" [Owen], 12° 31′ 42" [Douville], E long. 13° 23′ 30″ [Douville], 230 m. from Angola, near the mouth of the Catumbela, on an open bay formed to the SW by a projecting point of cliffs, above which rises Mount Sombreiro, or St. Philip's bonnet. It contains about 3,000 inhabitants, of whom one-third are Whites and Mulattoes. It is badly supplied with water, and its low site in "a marsh full of stagnant pools" [Owen], renders it peculiarly unhealthy; it has indeed been in contemplation for years to remove the seat of gov. to Mossamedes. The principal buildings in the town are the hospital, the church, and the assembly-house. Its chief defence is a large fort to the l. of the town, but which is fast mouldering to decay. It contains 28 honeycombed guns of small calibre. The principal trade of the place is in slaves, which the Portuguese merchants buy from the native chiefs for about 5 dollars a-head. When Dr. Tams visited B. in 1841, there was neither priest nor schoolmaster in the place.—Old B. stood on the coast, about 100 m. to the N of the new town of B. just described. It was ruined by the inroads of savage hordes from the interior; and its site is no longer traceable. The buildings, like those even of New B., having been erected of half-baked bricks, with mud for cement, and the whole coated by a thick plaster of shell-lime, have literally melted away under the rains, when the plaster has once given way. Authorities. Bowdich's Account of the Discoveries of the Portuguese in the interior of Angola, &c. Lond., rich and varied. Pine-apples, grapes, oranges, ba- 1824, 8vo. - Memorias contenendo a biographia do Vice Almiraute Luiz da Motta Feo e Torres. Paris, 8vo., 1825. — Douville Voyage au Congo. Paris, 1832. Tome i., chap. 2.—Tams' Visit to the Portuguese possessions in SW Africa. Translated from the German by Lloyd. Lond., 1845, 2 vols.—Narrative of Voyages to explore the shores of Africa, &c., under the direction of Capt. Owen. Lond., 1833, 2 vols 8vo.
BENGUET, a commune of France, in the dep. of Landes, and cant. of Grenade-sur-l'Adour. Pop. 1,288.
BENGWORTH, a parish in Worcestershire, adjoining Evesham, on the SE. Pop. 1,082.
BENHADLAF (ISAF and UCHAF), two townships in the p. of Llanrhaiadr-ym-Mochnant, in Denbighshire. Pop. of Isaf B. 207; of Uchaf B. 100.
BENHALL, a parish in Suffolk, 11 m. W by S of Saxmundham. Area 2,010 acres. Pop. 749.
BENHAM (Marsh), a tything in the p. of Speen, Berks, 3 m. W of Speenhamland. Pop. 316.
BENHAM (HOE), a tything in the p. of Welford, Berks. Pop. 337.
BENHOLME, a p. in Kincardineshire. Area 4,720 acres. Pop. in 1841, 1,648, of whom 1,172 were in the v. of Johnshaven.

BENHOPE, a noble mountain of Sutherlandshire, towering to the height of 3,150 ft. above sea-level. It extends in a SW direction along the vale of Strathmore in the parish of Durness. It may be approached by the road leading from the head of Loch Eribol to Loch Naver; or from the head of Loch Hope, which stretches from its western base towards Loch Eribol. It is composed of quartz and grey slate.
BENHORN, a mountain of Sutherlandshire, in the p. of Golspie. Alt. 1,712 ft.
BENI, or Paro. See Amazon, p. 199.

BENI, or VENI, a river of S America, which takes its rise in the mountain-chain to the SE of Lake Titicaca, in Upper Peru, runs in a N direction through the intendancy of La Paz; and thence curving to the NE, forms the line of separation between Upper and Lower Peru. In S lat. 11° 43', it divides into two branches, distinguished by the names of the Ferreiros and Madeira. These, on reaching the confines of Brazil, unite with the Mamorè, which thence assumes the appellation of Madeira. Its tributaries— the principal of which are the Quetebo and Tequieri on the r. bank, and on the l. the Pelechuco, Chulumani, and Uchupiamonas-form numerous inosculations with the great adjacent rivers.
BENIAKONI, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Grodno, in the district and 20 m. N of Lida.
BENI-ALI, or BENI-ADIN, a town of Upper Egypt, on an inosculation of the Nile, 5 m. SW of Manfalut, and 18 m. NW of Siut, on the route of caravans to Darfur.
BENI-ASEM, a town of Central Egypt, on an island of the Nile, 2 m. E of Samelud, and 17 m. SW of Abu Girgeh.
BENI-BESSERI, a town of Algiers, in the prov. of Segelmesa. The surrounding district is extremely tertile, and affords excellent pasturage. Its principal productions are corn and honey.
BENIC, a village of France, in the dep. of Nord, and cant. of Lanvollon, 7 m. NNW of St. Brieux. It possesses a small port.

BENICARLO, or Venicarlo, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Valencia, situated near the shore of the Mediterranean, 3 m. N of Pensacola, and 43 m. NE of Castillon-de-Plana, in N lat. 10° 11'. It is surrounded by walls and a ditch, and contains an old castle. The streets, although in some cases tolerably straight, are narrow and dirty; and the houses, notwithstanding the comparative affluence of the inhabitants, poor and ill-built. B. contains a parishchurch, a convent, and an hospital. It possesses an BEN active commerce in wines of local produce, which are exported to Bourdeaux. Pop. 5,500.

BENIDORME, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Valencia, on the shore of the Mediterranean, 28 m. NE of Alicante, and 4 m. SSW of Altea. It has some woollen manufactories, and possesses a small fishing-port. Its fishermen are deemed the best in the prov. Pop. 3,000.—There is a small island of the same name 2 m. S 1 E of the town.
BENIFALLET, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Valencia, near the l. bank of the Ebro, 14 m. NNE of Tortosa.
BENIGANIM, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Valencia, and 5 m. SE of San Felipe. The environs afford large quantities of wine. Pop. 4,000.
BENIGNE (St.), a commune of France, in the dep. of Ain, and cant. of Pont-de-Vaux. Pop. 1,224.

BENIGNO (SAN), a town of Piedmont, the cap. of a mand. of the same name, in the prov. and 12 m. NNE of Turin. It is celebrated for its abbey, the church of which is reputed one of the finest in Italy. Pop. 1,500.
BENIGUET, an island of France, in the Passage du Four, off the W coast of the dep. of Finisterre, in N lat. 48° 22′, W long. 4° 53′.
BENI-HAMED, a town of Central Egypt, near the I. bank of the Nile, in the prov. and 3 m. SSE of Minieh.

BENI-HASSAN, a village of Central Egypt, on the r. bank of the Nile, in the prov. and 15 m. SSE of Minieh, in N lat. 27° 53'. It occupies the site of the ancient Speos Artemidos. In the environs are numerous hypogeums, and catacombs excavated in a ridge of calcareous rock, and covered with curious paintings and hieroglyphical inscriptions. The paintings are highly illustrative of the arts and domestic economy of the ancient Egyptians.—A more modern v. of the same name appears to have grown up a little to the S of the old v.

BENI-HASSAN, or Hassen, a district of Marocco, stretching from the shore of the Atlantic inland between the Sebu and Bu Regreg rivers. It is exextremely fertile, producing grain in great abundance. Its pop. is stated to amount to 300,000. Its capital, Salee, is situated at the mouth of the Bu Regreg.
BENI-MAHOMED, a town of Upper Egypt, in the prov. and 10 m. NNW of Siut, on the r. bank of the Nile.
BENI-MERED, a military v. formed by the French in the district of Blidah, in Algiers, 8 m. SE of Blidah, and 4 m. from Buffarik. Pop. in 1845, 293.

BENI-MEZZAB, a district of N. Africa, in the Biled-el-Jerid, stretching between the southern range of the Atlas chain and the low sandy hills which form the northern boundary of the Sahara. Gardieh, the cap., is situated in N lat. 32° 45', and W long. 1° 35'. The pop. of the dist. consists of a tribe of Arabs bearing the same name.
BENIM-HAMED-EL-KUFUR, a town of Central Egypt, in the prov. of Benisuef, on the l. bank of the Nile, 10 m. SSW of Abu Girgeh.

BENIN, sometimes called GREAT BENIN, an extensive kingdom in Guinea; bounded on the N by Gago, Nigritia, and a chain of mountains; on the S by the gulf of Guinea; on the W by the kingdom of Ardra; and on the E by Mujak and Istanna. Its limits are little known; but it appears to comprehend all that part of the coast which stretches from the Rio Lagos to the Rio Formosa, an extent of 120 m. The whole coast presents a succession of riverestuaries, some of them very broad, but which were never explored by Europeans. These streams, ramifying and intersecting each other, form a labyrinth of alluvial islands; they even bring down small floating islets covered with shrubs and bushes. This aspect of the coast suggested to M. Reichard, a recent geographer, that these estuaries might form the embouchure of the Niger, the termination of which was, till lately, enveloped in mystery; and it was ascertained by the successful voyage of the Landers down the Niger or Quorra, from Boussa to the sea, that Reichard was correct in his conjecture; and that the Rio Formosa, or river of Benin, is the great NW branch of the Niger, which separates from the main river at a town called Anya, and forms the NW boundary of the delta of that celebrated stream. The whole coast SE of the river Benin, to the mouth of the Rio Nun at Cape Formosa, and from thence ENE to the mouth of the Rio del Rey, being intersected by the numerous mouths of the celebrated African river. The country of B. is low and flat, thickly covered with wood, and intersected in many places with rivers and small lakes. In some parts, however, particularly on the road from Agatton to Formosa, there is little or no water to be found; yet even there trees and plants grow to great perfection; and the whole country between Agatton and Formosa is adorned with orange and lemon trees. Cotton is the most abundant production. Though one of the richest kingdoms in Guinea, B. appears scarcely to yield any gold-dust, though it is found in almost every other district of the western coast of Africa. Its shores abound in fish of almost every kind, and are particularly celebrated for a species of blue coral, which forms a lucrative branch of trade. The animal kingdom comprises elephants, tigers, leopards, wild boars, civet cats, mountain cats, horses, hares, and a species of hairy fleeced sheep. The principal birds are parroquets, pigeons, partridges, storks, and ostriches. Crocodiles, sea-horses, and a particular species of torpedo, swarm in the rivers.—The climate is unwholesome. The pleasantest or rather the most tolerable season is in the months of August and September, when the air is refreshed by frequent rains. Violent thunder storms prevail during the months of June and July. But the most noxious season is in the months of October, November, and December; when the heat is intolerable, and the country is perpetually enveloped in thick pestilential fogs. One of the most prominent features in the character of the inhabitants of B., is their friendly and benevolent disposition. They are courteous and hospitable to strangers, and have many institutions which breathe the purest spirit of humanity; but indolence is unhappily an equally characteristic disposition amongst all their tribes. Only those whom poverty compels to work will submit to any kind of manual labour; the rest devolve upon their women and slaves the toil of cultivating the ground, and even the practice of the few arts with which they are acquainted. Next to cotton cloth, the most common productions of industry are mats, baskets, and spoons, and other instruments of ivory. Male children are accounted the property of the king; but the female are left to the disposal of their parents. Infants of both sexes are circumcised when a fortnight old. The funeral obsequies of their kings are celebrated in a frantic and barbarous manner. With the exception of the Portuguese, who have an insignificant factory at Awerri, the Dutch are the only Europeans who have had any establishments in the kingdom of B. Benin, the cap. of the above state, is situated upon the r. bank of the Formosa. Its extent, according to the reports of earlier travellers, appears to be immense. The circumference is said to be about 18 m.; the largest street is described by them as 3 m. long; and several others are of nearly equal dimensions. It cannot be doubted that a great part of this space is mere open ground, and that all the houses have a wide interval between them. The king's palace consists of a number of square enclosures, containing houses for the king himself, his women, and his officers, besides stables, magazines, and repositories of every description. A few figures in copper, but of such rude workmanship that it is difficult to discover whether men or beasts were meant to be represented by them, adorn this building. The wall of the town is composed of trunks of trees interwoven in the manner of a palisade, and the gates are of wood. Daily markets are held in the great streets; and separate quarters are allotted to each distinct species of merchandise. B. is described by Mr. Moffat and the late Mr. Smith as by no means extensive or important. The houses are of red clay, and similar to those of other towns in that part of Africa. Near the market-place the recent English travellers were shocked with the sight of a great collection of human sculls bleaching in the sun, and still more by seeing in the outskirts of the town, not far from the king's residence, the bodies of men who had been but recently decapitated, with Turkey-buzzards feeding on them, and on the roof of a hut close by, two corpses in a sitting posture. They experienced considerable difficulty in being admitted to the presence of the king, and were so only after complying with certain customs. There was much difficulty in conveying the information that England was governed by a queen; and it excited laughter when at last it was made known to the king and his attendants, through the interpreter, "that the king of England was a woman." The country between Gato and Benin city is finely wooded, and in some places very beautiful.

BENIN, a river of W Africa, which flows into the bight of Benin, in N lat. 5° 46', E long. 5° 1' [Owen], 116 m. NNW of Cape Formosa. It is the RIO FORmosa of the Portuguese. It is 13 m. wide at its mouth; a few miles from the sea it narrows to $ m.; and at New Town, which is 6 leagues up, is only between 400 and 500 yards in breadth; but it is at times wider or narrower. Near New Town it sends off a branch to the NE, which communicates with Gato, and is called Gato creek; and another which running SE, inosculates with the Rio Forcados or Warri, which in its turn inosculates with the Niger by a branch emerging from its r. bank, a little below Eboe. The main branch of the Benin has an ENE direction, but is not navigable above 50 m. for vessels of 50 tons. The tide in this river flows 6 hours at full and change, and rises 5 or 6 ft. During the existence of the slave-trade, this river was much resorted to by slavers; palm-oil and ivory are now the only articles exported from it in British ships. Reichard, as early as 1808, conjectured that this river would be found to be a deltoid branch of the Niger; and his opinion was espoused by Malte Brun and by M'Queen.

BENIN (BIGHT OF), a portion of the gulf of Guinea, on the Slave-coast of Africa, stretching from Cape Formosa near the mouth of the Rio Nun on the E, and Cape St. Paul near the mouth of the Rio Volta on the W. A line drawn between these points measures about 320 m.; but the depth of the bay, as measured by a perpendicular to this line, nowhere exceeds 90 m. The entire coast presents one continuous line of low, level, marshy shore, diversified only by detached clumps of trees, and intersected by a singular interlacement of creeks, lagunes, marshes, and streams,—the whole being "forbidding in its aspect, dangerous to approach, repulsive when examined, and disgusting when known." The W point of Terra Formosa, forming the E point of the estuary of the Nun, is in N lat. 4° 28', E long. 5° 41' 30". The land is here so low, though it receives the designation of a cape, that the trees upon it seem to shoot up out of the sea; and at 15 m. distance, neither trees nor land can be seen. At 6 m. or 7 m. from shore, the depth is 5 and 6 fath., on a muddy bettom. Passing the mouth of the Nun, at 20 m. N by W & W from Cape Formosa is the mouth of the Rio Dodo; and 20 m. N by W 1 W from the Dodo is the mouth of the Rio Ramos; 17 m. beyond which is the Rio Forcados, the entrance of which is in S lat. 5° 21'. On this last-mentioned river the Portuguese had a factory named Paloma, 5 leagues to the NE of which is the town of Warri, which sometimes gives name to the river. About 14 m. NW by N from the embouchure of the Forcados or Warri, is that of the Escravos or Slave river; and at the same distance from this last-mentioned river is the entrance of the Benin. All these rivers, from the Nun to the Benin, inosculate with each other in a singularly intricate manner, and the territory which they intersect is known as the kingdom of Warri or Warre; of which a great portion is covered with dense forests, and much with impenetrable marshes. The NW point of the entrance of the Benin, or Rio Formosa, is in N lat. 5° 46', E long. 5° 4'. Here the shore is sandy, and the water shallow, and of a dark green colour, with the current setting to the E and SE. From Benin to Lagos entrance, a distance of 29 leagues, the shore is low, inhospitable, and drowned by the heavy rains in August and September. It presents, however, two small towns called Oddy and Palma. A creek branching off from the W side of the Benin, near its mouth, runs nearly parallel to the shore, and only 3 m. distant, to the E end of Cradu lake or lagune, which extends 15 leagues from E to W, with an average breadth of 2 leagues. The river Lagos, coming from the N, falls into the W end of this lake, with a large volume of water, and forms a navigable channel to the sea. The town of Lagos stands on an island of the same name at the entrance of Cradu lake; and 10 leagues from its entrance, on the N shore, is the town of Cradu belonging to the kingdom of Jabu. From the Lagos to the river Badagry, a distance of 12 leagues, the coast is low and marshy, fringed with palm-trees, and washed by a tremendous surf. The entrance to the Badagry is narrow, and overshadowed with luxuriant vegetation, and on proceeding upwards it presents a beautiful lake-like expanse, with picturesque banks shaded by trees of lovely verdure. Porto Novo, the port of Ardrah, is 4 leagues to the W of Badagry; and 27 leagues to the W of Porto Novo, passing the small towns of Appee and Jackin, is Whydah, in S lat. 6° 19', E long. 2° 5'. A low and swampy coast, dotted with trees, intervenes between Whydah and Great Popoe, 41 leagues W by S from Whydah; and 71 leagues beyond this, is little Popoe, from which the coast rounds regularly a distance of 12 leagues to Quitta, which is 44 leagues to the NE of Cape St. Paul. The whole coast, from the mouth of the Badagry westward, to within a little distance of the Rio Volta, is bordered by an almost continuous lagune, which indeed may be regarded as a continuation of the Cradu lagune, to the E of the Badagry. This lagune is separated from the sea by a narrow beach, in some places not half-a-mile broad, and rarely exceeding 2 m., and it is kept constantly supplied with fresh water by the numerous large rivers which enter it. A surveying expedition is now employed in exploring this sheet of water, and the different river-branches which enter it. The dry season commences in the bight of B. in August and continues till January. In February and March tornadoes are of frequent occurrence. The great slave marts of W Africa exist on the line of coast now traced; and at one time this infamous trade was so active in the bight, and the demand and competition so great, that 20 vessels, or more, might be seen waiting at Whydah for their cargoes, and their stay was sometimes prolonged for a twelvemonth before they succeeded in completing their shipments. This trade has suffered a severe check, but still exists. Ivory, vegetable tallow, indigo, palm-oil, cotton, gums, wax, dye-woods, and ebony, might, it is believed, be obtained in great quantities by the trader along the shores of this bight, could the inducements to prosecute the slave-trade, on the part of the native chiefs, be annihilated, and crews of African blood obtained who might encounter the climate with impunity.

BENIN-D'AZY (SAINT), a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Nievre, arrond. of Nevers .- The cant. comprises 20 com., and contained in 1831 a pop. of 9,282.—The com. is 14 m. E of Nevers, and contains some blast furnaces and forges. Pop. 1,637.
BENING - LES - RORBACH, a commune of France, in the dep. of Moselle, and cant. of Rorbach. Pop. 1,213.
BENI-OBEID, a town of Central Egypt, in the prov. and 11 m. S of Minieh, on an affluent of the Nile.

BENI-OLEED, a town, or rather a series of straggling mud villages stretching along the sides of a fertile ravine for several miles, 6 days' SE of Tripoli. The inhabitants are Arabs of the Orfilly tribe. Dr. Oudney describes the wadi over which the huts are scattered as a rich valley, bounded on all sides by hills, whose height does not exceed 400 ft. Their structure is chiefly limestone capped with columnar greenstone.
BENISA, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Valencia, 40 m. NE of Alicante, and 12 m. S of Denia, near the shore of the Mediterranean.
BENISALEM, a town of the island of Majorca, BEN in the prov. and 15 m. NE of Palma, situated on a hill. Silk worms are extensively reared in the environs.
BENISANET, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Catalonia, 18 m. N of Tortosa, near the r. bank of the Ebro.
BENISCH, or Bentsch. See Bennisch.
BENISERILE, a town of Senegambia, cap. of the kingdom of Dintelia, 170 m. NNW of Timbo.
BENI SHEFRA, a town of Arabia, in the prov. of Hedjaz, on the caravan route from Mecca to Sada, and 35 m. SE of Tabala.
BENI SHEKIR, a town of Upper Egypt, in the prov. of Siut, on the l. bank of the Nile, 4 m. N of Manfalout.
BEN-ISPAHAN. See ISPAHUNUK.

BENISUF, or Benisuef, a prov. of Central Egypt, stretching along the western bank of the Nile, between Fayum on the N and Minieh on the S. It is divided into the two departments of Bash and Benisuef, one of the most fertile districts in Egypt.— Its cap., of the same name, is situated on the l. bank of the Nile, 65 m. S of Cairo. It is comparatively a large town, and has some manufactories of wool, besides one of the pasha's cotton-spinning mills. It occupies the site of the ancient Ptolemaidon.
BENI TAT, a town of Upper Egypt, in the prov. of Siut, on the l. bank of the Nile, 6 m. SE of Tahtah.
BENITES, a river of Venezuela, in the prov. of Coro, which falls into Lake Maracaibo, 35 m. SW of the town of that name.
BENITO, a river of Guinea, which falls into the bight of Biafra, in N lat. 1° 32'.
BENITO (SAN), an island off the coast of Lower California, at the entrance of the bay of Sebastian Viscamo, 20 m. W of the island of Cerros, in N lat. 28° 20′, and W long. 115° 45′.
BENI WASSEL, a nomadic Arab tribe who inhabit the immense desert tract to the E of the Nile, stretching from 27° to 28° 30′ N lat.
BENJARMASSIN. See Banjermassin.

BENJOAR, or BANJOAR, an island of the Asiatic archipelago, to the SW of the island of Savu, in S lat. 10° 35′, and E long. 121° 40′. Its greatest length is 15 m., and its height is considerable. It is generally well-wooded, and is inhabited.
BENKEN, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Basle, 4 m. NNW of Andelfingen. Pop. 332.

BENLAWERS, a mountain of Perthshire, in the p. of Kenmore, on the NE side of Loch Tay. Its alt. is stated by some at 3,944 ft.; by others, at 4,015 ft. above sea-level. It is easy of ascent,—so much so, that one may ride to the summit. Benlomond probably can alone compete with this mountain for the grandeur of the view to be obtained from it; but a greater variety, and a greater range of country can be seen from Benlawers; and it has this advantage, that it towers over all the neighbouring mountains, by more than 1,000 ft. Bencruachan and Buachaille-Etive are particularly conspicuous from it. To the N are Shechallion and its adjoining mountains, and in this direction the eye is carried as far as Gleneoe and Bennevis, on the one hand; while, on the other, Benygloe lifts its complicated summit above the head of Ferrogon; and, beyond this, the mountains of Marr and of Cairngorm, at the head of the Dee, some of them marked with perpetual snow, can be traced. This mountain presents a most interesting field to the botanist.

BENLEDI, a mountain of Perthshire, 2 m. W of Callander, rising to 3,009 ft., according to some; but according to others-and more correctly we believe -to 2,863 ft. above sea-level. It commands an extensive prospect of Stirlingshire and of the windings of the Forth.

BENLEVAGH, a mountain on the NE border of co. Galway. It rises, in a stupendous mass, on the frontier of Joyce-country, and occupies a large part of the broad isthmus between loughs Mask and Corrib. Its alt. is 1,370 ft.
BENLI, or Begli, a town of Turkey in Europe, in Romelia, on a tributary of the Rokludze, 8 m. SSW of Aidos, and 15 m. WNW of Burgas.
BENLLACH, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Valencia, 11 m. N of Castillon-de-la-Plana.

BENLOMOND, a mountain on the eastern bank of Lochlomond, in the p. of Buchanan, in Stirlingshire, at the SW extremity of the Grampian chain. Its alt. has been variously estimated at 3,262, 3,175, and 3,091 ft. We are inclined to adopt the second of these admeasurements, which is that of Mr. Galbraith. [Edin. New Phil. Journ., vol. vi., p. 126.] The N side of this mountain presents an aspect peculiarly terrific as beheld from the summit. Here the mighty mass, which hitherto had appeared to be an irregular cone placed on a spreading base, suddenly presents itself as an imperfect crater, with one side forcibly torn off, leaving a stupendous precipice of 2,000 ft., from which most travellers recoil with terror. In the summer months, this mountain is visited by strangers from every quarter of the island, as well as foreigners, who come to view the romantic scenery of the Highlands; the month of September is in general accounted the best for ascending it, because from the cool temperature of the air, the horizon is then less clouded by vapours than during the more intense heats of summer. B. is chiefly composed of granite, interspersed with great quantities of quartz. This last mineral is found near the top, in immense masses, some of which must weigh several tons; these appear like patches of snow upon the mountain, when seen from Luss, on the opposite side of the lake. Considerable quantities of micaceous schistus are found, even at the top; and many rocks towards the base of the mountain are entirely composed of this mineral. Plovers abound near the middle of the mountain; grouse are found a little higher; and near the top ptarmigans are occasionally seen.

BENLUNDIE, a mountain of Sutherlandshire, in the p. of Golspie. Alt. 1,464 ft. BENMACDHU, BENNAMACDUICH, OF BENMACDHUIE, one of the Cairngorm group of mountains, in the SW corner of Aberdeenshire, estimated by Jameson at 4.300 ft. in alt.; by Mr. H. C. Watson at 4,326 ft.; and by others at 4,390 ft. If this latter admeasurement be correct, this mountain must be higher than Bennevis, hitherto regarded as the most elevated spot in Great Britain. B., stretching over a considerable space, has many summits, and presents a vast variety of aspects; but towards the NE, where it turns itself into a basin, joining the contiguous summits of Benan and Benabuird, it assumes a form peculiarly striking and grim. Here one rock distinguishes itself from its brethren by displaying a pointed needle from a summit of vast height, which appears considerably off the perpendicular, and hangs its head over the glen below. Mr. Macpherson Grant of Ballindalloch, in a communication to the editor of the Edin. New Phil. Journ., gives an interesting account of an atmospheric appearance resembling that of the far-famed spectre of the Brocken, witnessed by him on this mountain on the 10th Oct., 1830, at 3 P. M.

BENMORE, the highest mountain in Mull. It lies between the head of Loch-na-keal and Loch Screidan. Macculloch says: "The ascent is neither very tedious nor difficult. I found it to be 3,097 ft. high. The view is various and extensive. Staffa, Iona, the Treshinish isles, Coll and Tirey, with Ulva, Gometra, Colonsa, Eorsa, and other objects, are seen BEN beautifully diversifying the broad face of the western sea, distinct as in a map, while, to the southward, Scarba and Jura, with the smaller isles of the Argyllshire coast, recede gradually in the distant haze. The rugged surface of Mull itself excludes the objects to the eastward; but Loch Screidan forms a beautiful picture beneath our feet, its long and bright bay deeply intersecting with its dazzling surface the troubled heap of mountains."
BENMORE, a village in the p. of Rattoo, co. Kerry. Pop. in 1831, 448.
BENNAU, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. and 9 m. NNE of Schwyz.
BENNEBROEK, a town of Holland, in the prov. of that name, on the W side of Haarlem Meer, 4 m. S of Haarlem.

BENNECKSTEIN, or BENNECKENSTEIN, a town of Prussia, in a detached district of the prov. of Saxony, in the circle and 12 m. NNW of Nordhausen, on the Rap-Bode, at the base of the Hartz mountains, at an alt. of 1,560 ft. above sea-level. It has extensive manufactories of hardware and turnery. Pop. in 1837, 3,362.
BENNECOURT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Seine-et-Oise, and cant. of Bonnieres. Pop. 1,145.
BENNEKOM, a town of Holland, in the prov. of Gelderland, 9 m. WNW of Arnheim.
BENNETT'S BRIDGE, a village in co. Kilkenny, on the Nore river, 5 m. SW by S of Gowran. Pop. 263.
BENNETSVILLE, a village of South Carolina, U. S., the cap. of Marlboro district, 107 m. NE of Columbia. Pop. in 1840, about 450.

BENNEVIS, a mountain in the SW extremity of Inverness-shire, in 56° 49' N lat, 4° 40' W long., having an alt., according to Jameson, of 4,380 ft. It is exceeded, geometrically, by Benmacdhu; and it must be remembered that Bennevis is a much more independent mountain; and that, on the W side at least, it rises almost immediately from a plain which is nearly on a level with the sea. Hence it is, in reality, still the highest mountain in Scotland, though not the most elevated ground; while its effect to the eye is far more striking than that of any other, -all the rival elevations either springing from high land, or being entangled among other hills so as to lose their consequence. Its form is, at the same time, heavy and graceless, and very peculiar, as if one mountain had been placed on another; and this effect, as of a casual and posterior addition, is rendered still more striking by the difference in outline and character between the two portions. This appearance, so remarkable to the ordinary spectator, is easily explained by the geologist; who finds that the lower portion is formed of granite and schistose rocks, and that the upper is a mass of porphyry.
BENNINGEN, a town of Wurtemburg, in the circle of Neckar, 4 m. NNE of Ludwigsburg, on the Neckar. Pop. 897. The remains of an ancient Roman town existed, in 1597, in this locality.
BENNINGHOLME, a township in the p. of Swine, E. R. of Yorkshire, 7 m. E by S of Beverley. Area 1,280 acres. Pop. 108.
BENNINGTON, a parish in Hertfordshire, 5 m. ESE of Stevenage. Area 2,950 acres. Pop. 605.— Also a p. in Lincolnshire, 5 m. NE of Boston. Area 3,090 acres. Pop. 539.

BENNINGTON, a county of Vermont, U. S., in the SW corner of the state, and comprising a superficial area of 610 sq. m. It is generally hilly, and on the E extends into the range of the Green mountains. The western districts are tolerably fertile. A limestone ridge, by which the co. is intersected from N to S, affords marble and iron-ore. Pop. in 1830, 17,470; in 1840, 16,872.—Also a village, semi-capital with Manchester of the forenamed co., 57 m. NE of Albany, 117 m. SW of Montpelier, settled in 1761. It is intersected by branches of the Hoosick, which afford good water-power. The surrounding district is fertile, and affords marble, iron-ore, and yellow ochre. Pop. in 1840, 3,429.—Also a township of New York, in Wyoming co., 264 m. W of Albany. It has an undulating surface, and is watered by the Cayuga and Tonawanda creeks. Its soil consists of moist clay, and is generally fertile. Pop. in 1840, 2,368. -Also a town of Michigan, in Shiawassee co., watered by the Looking-Glass. Pop. in 1840, 403.—Also a township of Ohio, in Delaware co., 40 m. NE of Columbus. Pop. in 1840, 1,051.
BENNINGTON (Long), a parish in Lincolnshire, 7 m. NW of Grantham. Area 4,420 acres. Pop. 1,007.
BENNISCH, or BENDSCHIN, a town of Moravia, in the circle and 15 m. WNW of Troppau. It contains linen manufactories, and in the vicinity are some iron-works. Pop. 2,605.
BENNIWORTH, a parish in Lincolnshire, 6 m. E by N of Wragby. Area 2,840 acres. Pop. 488.

BENNSHAUSEN, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of Saxony, circle of Schleusingen, 11 m. NE of Meinungen, and 31 m. SW of Erfurth. It possesses an iron-work, and has considerable commerce in wine. Pop. 1,532.
BENNWIHR, a commune of France, in the dep. of Upper Rhine, and cant. of Kaisersberg. Pop. 1,025.
BENO, a small island of France, in the dep. of Cotes-du-Nord, and in the cant. and com. of Perros- Guirec. Pop. 60.

BENODET, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Finisterre, cant. of Fouesnant, and com. of Perguet, on a bay of the same name, at the mouth of the Odet. It possesses a small port, which affords good anchorage. Its shipping in 1837 amounted to 2,151 tons. Pop. 670.

BENOIST-DU-SAULT (SAINT), a canton, commane, and town of France, in the dep. of Indre, arrond. of Le Blanc.—The cant. comprises 14 com., and contained, in 1831, a pop. of 12,641.—The town is pleasantly situated on a small stream, 23 m. SE of Le Blanc. It contains a communal college, and possesses some blast-furnaces and forges. Goats' skins form its chief article of commerce. In the vicinity are the celebrated rocks and cascades of Montgerno. Pop. 1,243.

BENOIT (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Vosges, and cant. of Rambervillers. Pop. 1,039.—Also a commune and town of the island of Bourbon, one of the six quarters or parishes of the district of Vent, on the E coast, at the mouth of the Marsouins, 27 m. SE of St. Denis. The houses, although only one story in height, are generally spacious, and are surrounded with gardens. The church is finely placed on a rising ground. A wooden-bridge, 200 ft. in length, and remarkable for the simplicity of construction, crosses the river. The estuary or bay forms a harbour for small vessels. The environs produce sugar, coffee, nutmegs, cloves, and some other spices. Pop. in 1836, 11,376, of whom 7,416 were slaves.
